			<li id="t-whatlinkshere" class="mw-list-item"><a href="/wiki/Special:WhatLinksHere/Black_hole" title="List of all English Wikipedia pages containing links to this page [j]" accesskey="j"><span>What links here</span></a></li><li id="t-recentchangeslinked" class="mw-list-item"><a href="/wiki/Special:RecentChangesLinked/Black_hole" rel="nofollow" title="Recent changes in pages linked from this page [k]" accesskey="k"><span>Related changes</span></a></li><li id="t-upload" class="mw-list-item"><a href="//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:File_Upload_Wizard" title="Upload files [u]" accesskey="u"><span>Upload file</span></a></li><li id="t-permalink" class="mw-list-item"><a href="/w/index.php?title=Black_hole&amp;oldid=1347759629" title="Permanent link to this revision of this page"><span>Permanent link</span></a></li><li id="t-info" class="mw-list-item"><a href="/w/index.php?title=Black_hole&amp;action=info" title="More information about this page"><span>Page information</span></a></li><li id="t-cite" class="mw-list-item"><a href="/w/index.php?title=Special:CiteThisPage&amp;page=Black_hole&amp;id=1347759629&amp;wpFormIdentifier=titleform" title="Information on how to cite this page"><span>Cite this page</span></a></li><li id="t-urlshortener" class="mw-list-item"><a href="/w/index.php?title=Special:UrlShortener&amp;url=https%3A%2F%2Fen.wikipedia.org%2Fwiki%2FBlack_hole"><span>Get shortened URL</span></a></li>

					<div id="contentSub"><div id="mw-content-subtitle"></div></div>
					
					
					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Compact astronomical body</div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1320445320">.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}</style><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">For other uses, see <a href="/wiki/Black_hole_(disambiguation)" class="mw-disambig" title="Black hole (disambiguation)">Black hole (disambiguation)</a>.</div>
<p class="mw-empty-elt">



</p>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti">.mw-parser-output .tmulti .multiimageinner{display:flex;flex-direction:column}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .trow{display:flex;flex-direction:row;clear:left;flex-wrap:wrap;width:100%;box-sizing:border-box}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .tsingle{margin:1px;float:left}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .theader{clear:both;font-weight:bold;text-align:center;align-self:center;background-color:transparent;width:100%}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .thumbcaption{background-color:transparent}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .text-align-left{text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .text-align-right{text-align:right}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .text-align-center{text-align:center}@media all and (max-width:720px){.mw-parser-output .tmulti .thumbinner{width:100%!important;box-sizing:border-box;max-width:none!important;align-items:center}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .trow{justify-content:center}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .tsingle{float:none!important;max-width:100%!important;box-sizing:border-box;text-align:center}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .tsingle .thumbcaption{text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .tmulti .trow>.thumbcaption{text-align:center}}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .tmulti .multiimageinner span:not(.skin-invert-image):not(.skin-invert):not(.bg-transparent) img{background-color:white}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .tmulti .multiimageinner span:not(.skin-invert-image):not(.skin-invert):not(.bg-transparent) img{background-color:white}}</style><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:262px;max-width:262px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:260px;max-width:260px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:258px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Black_hole_-_Messier_87_crop_max_res.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A black spot, center, surrounded by a doughnut-shaped orange-yellow ring with the blackness of empty space in the background" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Black_hole_-_Messier_87_crop_max_res.jpg/330px-Black_hole_-_Messier_87_crop_max_res.jpg" decoding="async" width="258" height="258" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Black_hole_-_Messier_87_crop_max_res.jpg/960px-Black_hole_-_Messier_87_crop_max_res.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4320" data-file-height="4320" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption text-align-left">An image of the core region of <a href="/wiki/Messier_87" title="Messier 87">Messier 87</a>, a <a href="/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole" title="Supermassive black hole">supermassive black hole</a>, processed from a sparse array of <a href="/wiki/Radio_telescopes" class="mw-redirect" title="Radio telescopes">radio telescopes</a> known as the <a href="/wiki/Event_Horizon_Telescope" title="Event Horizon Telescope">EHT</a> with colours indicating <a href="/wiki/Brightness_temperature" title="Brightness temperature">brightness temperature</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-eht19&#95;1-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eht19-1"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:260px;max-width:260px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:206px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:BH_LMC.png" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A black circle, center, surrounded by three bright white blobs of light and the orange-coloured Milky Way distorted into an ark around it. The background is a starry sky with a second image of the Milky Way, not warped, visible in the top left corner." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/BH_LMC.png/330px-BH_LMC.png" decoding="async" width="258" height="206" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/BH_LMC.png/960px-BH_LMC.png 2x" data-file-width="2560" data-file-height="2048" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption text-align-left">Simulated view of a <a href="/wiki/Schwarzschild_black_hole" class="mw-redirect" title="Schwarzschild black hole">nonspinning, uncharged black hole</a> in front of the <a href="/wiki/Large_Magellanic_Cloud" title="Large Magellanic Cloud">Large Magellanic Cloud</a>. The <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_lensing" class="mw-redirect" title="Gravitational lensing">gravitational lensing</a> effect produces two enlarged but distorted views of the Cloud. Across the top, the <a href="/wiki/Milky_Way" title="Milky Way">Milky Way</a> disk appears distorted into an arc.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-riazuelo&#95;3-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-riazuelo-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div></div></div>
<p>A <b>black hole</b> is an <a href="/wiki/Astronomical_body" class="mw-redirect" title="Astronomical body">astronomical body</a> so <a href="/wiki/Compact_object" title="Compact object">compact</a> that its gravity prevents anything, including light, from escaping. <a href="/wiki/Albert_Einstein" title="Albert Einstein">Albert Einstein</a>'s theory of <a href="/wiki/General_relativity" title="General relativity">general relativity</a>, which describes gravitation as the <a href="/wiki/Curved_spacetime" title="Curved spacetime">curvature of spacetime</a>, predicts that any sufficiently compact <a href="/wiki/Mass" title="Mass">mass</a> will form a black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NYT-20150608&#95;4-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYT-20150608-4"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Boundary_(topology)" title="Boundary (topology)">boundary</a> of no escape is called the <a href="/wiki/Event_horizon" title="Event horizon">event horizon</a>. In general relativity, crossing a black hole's event horizon traps an object inside but produces no locally detectable change. General relativity also predicts that every black hole should have a central <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_singularity" title="Gravitational singularity">singularity</a>, where the <a href="/wiki/Curvature_of_spacetime" class="mw-redirect" title="Curvature of spacetime">curvature of spacetime</a> is infinite.
</p><p>Objects whose <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_field" title="Gravitational field">gravitational fields</a> are too strong for light to escape were first considered in the 18th century. In 1916, the first solution of general relativity that would characterise a black hole was found. By the late 1950s, this solution began to be interpreted physically as a region of space from which nothing can escape. Black holes were long considered a mathematical curiosity; it was not until the 1960s that theoretical work showed they were a generic prediction of general relativity. The first widely-accepted black hole was <a href="/wiki/Cygnus_X-1" title="Cygnus X-1">Cygnus X-1</a>, identified by several researchers independently in 1971.
</p><p>Black holes typically form when <a href="/wiki/Supernova" title="Supernova">very massive stars collapse</a> at the end of their <a href="/wiki/Stellar_evolution" title="Stellar evolution">life cycle</a>. After a black hole has formed, it can grow by absorbing mass from its surroundings. Supermassive black holes of millions of <a href="/wiki/Solar_mass" title="Solar mass">solar masses</a> may form by absorbing stars and merging with other black holes, or via <a href="/wiki/Direct_collapse_black_hole" title="Direct collapse black hole">direct collapse</a> of <a href="/wiki/Gas_cloud" class="mw-redirect" title="Gas cloud">gas clouds</a>. There is consensus that <a href="/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole" title="Supermassive black hole">supermassive black holes</a> exist in the centres of most <a href="/wiki/Galaxies" class="mw-redirect" title="Galaxies">galaxies</a>.
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Quantum_field_theory_in_curved_spacetime" title="Quantum field theory in curved spacetime">Quantum field theory in curved spacetime</a> predicts that event horizons emit <a href="/wiki/Hawking_radiation" title="Hawking radiation">Hawking radiation</a>, with its rate of emission being inversely proportional to its mass. This causes the black hole to lose mass very slowly, provided it is not <a href="/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics)" title="Accretion (astrophysics)">accreting</a> matter. However, even the smallest class of black holes observed, <a href="/wiki/Stellar_black_hole" title="Stellar black hole">stellar black holes</a>, are gaining mass from the <a href="/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background" title="Cosmic microwave background">cosmic microwave background</a> faster than they are losing mass via Hawking radiation.
</p><p>The presence of a black hole can be inferred through its interaction with <a href="/wiki/Matter" title="Matter">matter</a> and <a href="/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation" title="Electromagnetic radiation">electromagnetic radiation</a> such as visible light. Matter falling toward a black hole can form an <a href="/wiki/Accretion_disk" title="Accretion disk">accretion disk</a> of infalling plasma, heated by <a href="/wiki/Friction" title="Friction">friction</a> and emitting light. In extreme cases, this creates a <a href="/wiki/Quasar" title="Quasar">quasar</a>, some of the brightest objects in the universe. Merging black holes can be <a href="/wiki/Interferometry" title="Interferometry">detected</a> by the <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_wave" title="Gravitational wave">gravitational waves</a> they emit. If stars are orbiting a black hole, their motions can be used to determine the black hole's mass and location. In this way, astronomers have identified numerous stellar black hole candidates in <a href="/wiki/Binary_star" title="Binary star">binary systems</a> and established that the radio source known as <a href="/wiki/Sagittarius_A*" title="Sagittarius A*">Sagittarius A*</a>, at the core of the <a href="/wiki/Milky_Way" title="Milky Way">Milky Way</a> galaxy, contains a supermassive black hole of about 4.3<span class="nowrap">&#160;</span>million <a href="/wiki/Solar_mass" title="Solar mass">solar masses</a>.
</p>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r886046785">.mw-parser-output .toclimit-2 .toclevel-1 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-3 .toclevel-2 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-4 .toclevel-3 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-5 .toclevel-4 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-6 .toclevel-5 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-7 .toclevel-6 ul{display:none}</style><div class="toclimit-4"><meta property="mw:PageProp/toc" /></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="History">History</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/History_of_black_hole_physics" title="History of black hole physics">History of black hole physics</a></div>
<p>The idea of a body so massive that even light could not escape was first proposed in the late 18th century by English astronomer and clergyman <a href="/wiki/John_Michell" title="John Michell">John Michell</a> and independently by French scientist <a href="/wiki/Pierre-Simon_Laplace" title="Pierre-Simon Laplace">Pierre-Simon Laplace</a>. Both scholars proposed very large stars in contrast to the modern concept of an extremely dense object.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-origin&#95;5-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-origin-5"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Michell's idea, in a short part of a letter published in 1784,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-6"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> calculated that a star with the same density but 500 times the radius of the sun would not let any emitted light escape; the surface <a href="/wiki/Escape_velocity" title="Escape velocity">escape velocity</a> would exceed the <a href="/wiki/Speed_of_light" title="Speed of light">speed of light</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 122">&#58;&#8202;122&#8202;</span></sup> Michell correctly hypothesized that such non-radiating bodies might be detectable through their gravitational effects on nearby visible bodies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-origin&#95;5-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-origin-5"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 1796, while speculating on the origin of the Solar System in his book <span title="French-language text"><i lang="fr">Exposition du Système du Monde</i></span>, Laplace made a qualitative suggestion that a star could be invisible if it were sufficiently large. <a href="/wiki/Franz_Xaver_von_Zach" title="Franz Xaver von Zach">Franz Xaver von Zach</a> asked Laplace for a mathematical analysis, which Laplace provided and published in von Zach's journal <i><a href="/w/index.php?title=Allgemeine_Geographische_Ephemeriden&amp;action=edit&amp;redlink=1" class="new" title="Allgemeine Geographische Ephemeriden (page does not exist)">Allgemeine Geographische Ephemeriden</a><span class="noprint" style="font-size:85%; font-style: normal;">&#160;&#91;<a href="https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allgemeine_Geographische_Ephemeriden" class="extiw" title="de:Allgemeine Geographische Ephemeriden">de</a>&#93;</span></i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-origin&#95;5-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-origin-5"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="General_relativity">General relativity</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/History_of_general_relativity" title="History of general relativity">History of general relativity</a></div>

href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333133064" /><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1239400231">.mw-parser-output .navbar{display:inline;font-size:88%;font-weight:normal}.mw-parser-output .navbar-collapse{float:left;text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .navbar-boxtext{word-spacing:0}.mw-parser-output .navbar ul{display:inline-block;white-space:nowrap;line-height:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-brackets::before{margin-right:-0.125em;content:"[ "}.mw-parser-output .navbar-brackets::after{margin-left:-0.125em;content:" ]"}.mw-parser-output .navbar li{word-spacing:-0.125em}.mw-parser-output .navbar a>span,.mw-parser-output .navbar a>abbr{text-decoration:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-mini abbr{font-variant:small-caps;border-bottom:none;text-decoration:none;cursor:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-ct-full{font-size:114%;margin:0 7em}.mw-parser-output .navbar-ct-mini{font-size:114%;margin:0 4em}html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .navbar li a abbr{color:var(--color-base)!important}@media(prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .navbar li a abbr{color:var(--color-base)!important}}@media print{.mw-parser-output .navbar{display:none!important}}</style><div class="navbar plainlinks hlist navbar-mini"><ul><li class="nv-view"><a href="/wiki/Template:General_relativity_sidebar" title="Template:General relativity sidebar"><abbr title="View this template">v</abbr></a></li><li class="nv-talk"><a href="/wiki/Template_talk:General_relativity_sidebar" title="Template talk:General relativity sidebar"><abbr title="Discuss this template">t</abbr></a></li><li class="nv-edit"><a href="/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:General_relativity_sidebar" title="Special:EditPage/Template:General relativity sidebar"><abbr title="Edit this template">e</abbr></a></li></ul></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>In 1905, <a href="/wiki/Albert_Einstein" title="Albert Einstein">Albert Einstein</a> showed that the laws of <a href="/wiki/Electromagnetism" title="Electromagnetism">electromagnetism</a> are identical for observers travelling at different velocities relative to each other. The laws of <a href="/wiki/Mechanics" title="Mechanics">mechanics</a> had already been shown to be invariant in this way. However, the theory of gravitation was yet to be included.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Weinberg-1972&#95;8-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Weinberg-1972-8"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 19">&#58;&#8202;19&#8202;</span></sup> 
</p><p>In 1907, Einstein published a paper proposing his <a href="/wiki/Equivalence_principle" title="Equivalence principle">equivalence principle</a>, the hypothesis that <a href="/wiki/Inertial_mass" class="mw-redirect" title="Inertial mass">inertial mass</a> and <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_mass" class="mw-redirect" title="Gravitational mass">gravitational mass</a> have a common cause. Using the principle, Einstein predicted the <a href="/wiki/Redshift" title="Redshift">redshift</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_lensing" class="mw-redirect" title="Gravitational lensing">lensing</a> effect of gravity on light; his prediction of gravitational lensing was one-half of the value that the full theory of general relativity would predict.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Weinberg-1972&#95;8-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Weinberg-1972-8"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 19">&#58;&#8202;19&#8202;</span></sup> By 1915, Einstein refined these ideas into his <a href="/wiki/General_theory_of_relativity" class="mw-redirect" title="General theory of relativity">general theory of relativity</a>, which explained how matter affects spacetime, which in turn affects the motion of other matter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-9"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This formed the basis for black hole physics.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-11"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Singular_solutions_in_general_relativity">Singular solutions in general relativity</h3></div>
<p>Only a few months after Einstein published the <a href="/wiki/Einstein_field_equations" title="Einstein field equations">field equations</a> describing general relativity, astrophysicist <a href="/wiki/Karl_Schwarzschild" title="Karl Schwarzschild">Karl Schwarzschild</a> set out to apply the idea to stars. He assumed spherical symmetry with no spin and found a <a href="/wiki/Schwarzschild_metric" title="Schwarzschild metric">solution</a> to Einstein's equations.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 124">&#58;&#8202;124&#8202;</span></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Schwarzschild1916&#95;12-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Schwarzschild1916-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A few months after Schwarzschild, <a href="/wiki/Johannes_Droste" title="Johannes Droste">Johannes Droste</a>, a student of <a href="/wiki/Hendrik_Lorentz" title="Hendrik Lorentz">Hendrik Lorentz</a>, independently gave the same solution.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-13" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-13"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-14" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-14"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At a certain radius from the center of the mass, the Schwarzschild solution became <a href="/wiki/Singularity_(mathematics)" title="Singularity (mathematics)">singular</a>, meaning that some of the terms in the Einstein equations became infinite. The nature of this radius, which later became known as the <a href="/wiki/Schwarzschild_radius" title="Schwarzschild radius">Schwarzschild radius</a>, was not understood at the time.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-HooftHist&#95;15-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HooftHist-15"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many physicists of the early 20th century were sceptical of the existence of black holes. In a 1926 popular science book, <a href="/wiki/Arthur_Eddington" title="Arthur Eddington">Arthur Eddington</a> critiqued the idea of a star with mass compressed to its Schwarzschild radius as a flaw in the then-poorly-understood theory of general relativity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-eddington1926&#95;16-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eddington1926-16"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap">&#58;&#8202;<span title="Page / location: 134&#10;Quotation: &quot;The first conclusion was the Newtonian version of light not escaping; the second was a semi-accurate, relativistic description; and the third was typical Eddingtonian hyperbole ... when a star is as small as the critical circumference, the curvature is strong but not infinite, and space is definitely not wrapped around the star. Eddington may have known this, but his description made a good story, and it captured in a whimsical way the spirit of Schwarzschild&#39;s spacetime curvature.&quot;" class="tooltip tooltip-dashed" style="border-bottom: 1px dashed;">134</span>&#8202;</sup> In 1939, Einstein used his theory of general relativity in an attempt to prove that black holes were impossible.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bernstein-2007&#95;17-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bernstein-2007-17"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-18" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-18"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> His work relied on increasing pressure or increasing centrifugal force balancing the force of gravity so that the object would not collapse beyond its Schwarzschild radius. He missed the possibility that implosion would drive the system below this critical value.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 135">&#58;&#8202;135&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Gravity_vs_degeneracy_pressure">Gravity vs degeneracy pressure</h3></div>
<p>By the 1920s, astronomers had classified a number of <a href="/wiki/White_dwarf_stars" class="mw-redirect" title="White dwarf stars">white dwarf stars</a> as too cool and dense to be explained by the gradual cooling of ordinary stars. In 1926, <a href="/wiki/Ralph_Fowler" title="Ralph Fowler">Ralph Fowler</a> showed that these stars are not like <a href="/wiki/Main-sequence_star" class="mw-redirect" title="Main-sequence star">main-sequence stars</a>, where thermal pressure balances gravity. Instead, a type of <a href="/wiki/Degeneracy_pressure" class="mw-redirect" title="Degeneracy pressure">quantum-mechanical pressure</a> balances gravity at these temperatures and densities.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 145">&#58;&#8202;145&#8202;</span></sup> In 1931, <a href="/wiki/Subrahmanyan_Chandrasekhar" title="Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar">Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar</a> studied the new <a href="/wiki/State_of_matter" title="State of matter">state of matter</a> that results from this balance, called <a href="/wiki/Electron-degenerate_matter" class="mw-redirect" title="Electron-degenerate matter">electron-degenerate matter</a>, discovering that it is stable below a certain <a href="/wiki/Chandrasekhar_limit" title="Chandrasekhar limit">limiting mass</a>. By 1934 he showed that this explained the catalogue of white dwarf stars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 151">&#58;&#8202;151&#8202;</span></sup> When Chandrasekhar announced his results, Eddington pointed out that stars above this limit would radiate until they were sufficiently dense to prevent light from exiting, a conclusion he considered absurd. Eddington and, later, <a href="/wiki/Lev_Landau" title="Lev Landau">Lev Landau</a> argued that some yet unknown mechanism would stop the collapse.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-19" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-19"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the 1930s, <a href="/wiki/Fritz_Zwicky" title="Fritz Zwicky">Fritz Zwicky</a> and <a href="/wiki/Walter_Baade" title="Walter Baade">Walter Baade</a> studied <a href="/wiki/Stellar_nova" class="mw-redirect" title="Stellar nova">stellar novae</a>, focusing on exceptionally bright ones they called <a href="/wiki/Supernova" title="Supernova">supernovae</a>. Zwicky promoted the idea that supernovae produced stars with the density of atomic nuclei—<a href="/wiki/Neutron_stars" class="mw-redirect" title="Neutron stars">neutron stars</a>—but this idea was largely ignored at the time.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 171">&#58;&#8202;171&#8202;</span></sup> In 1939, based on Chandrasekhar's reasoning, <a href="/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer" title="J. Robert Oppenheimer">J. Robert Oppenheimer</a> and <a href="/wiki/George_Volkoff" title="George Volkoff">George Volkoff</a> predicted that neutron stars below a certain mass limit, later called the <a href="/wiki/Tolman%E2%80%93Oppenheimer%E2%80%93Volkoff_limit" title="Tolman–Oppenheimer–Volkoff limit">Tolman–Oppenheimer–Volkoff limit</a>, would be stable due to <a href="/wiki/Neutron_degeneracy_pressure" class="mw-redirect" title="Neutron degeneracy pressure">neutron degeneracy pressure</a>. Above that limit, they reasoned that either their model would not apply or that gravitational contraction would not stop.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-OV1939&#95;20-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-OV1939-20"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap">&#58;&#8202;<span title="Page: 380&#10;Quotation: &quot;There would then seem to be only two answers possible to the question of the behaviour of very massive stars: either the equation of state we have used so far fails to describe the behaviour of highly condensed matter that the conclusions reached above are qualitatively misleading, or the star will continue to contract indefinitely, never reaching equilibrium.&quot;" class="tooltip tooltip-dashed" style="border-bottom: 1px dashed;">380</span>&#8202;</sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/John_Archibald_Wheeler" title="John Archibald Wheeler">John Archibald Wheeler</a> and two of his students resolved questions about the model behind the Tolman–Oppenheimer–Volkoff (TOV) limit. In 1965, Harrison and Wheeler developed the <a href="/wiki/Equations_of_state" class="mw-redirect" title="Equations of state">equations of state</a> relating density to pressure for cold matter all the way through electron degeneracy and neutron degeneracy. Masami Wakano and Wheeler then used the equations to compute the equilibrium curve for stars, relating mass to circumference. They found no additional features that would invalidate the TOV limit. This meant that the only thing that could prevent black holes from forming was a dynamic process ejecting sufficient mass from a star as it cooled.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 205">&#58;&#8202;205&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Birth_of_modern_model">Birth of modern model</h3></div>
<p>The modern concept of black holes was formulated by <a href="/wiki/Robert_Oppenheimer" class="mw-redirect" title="Robert Oppenheimer">Robert Oppenheimer</a> and his student <a href="/wiki/Hartland_Snyder" title="Hartland Snyder">Hartland Snyder</a> in 1939.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bernstein-2007&#95;17-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bernstein-2007-17"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bartusiak&#95;21-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bartusiak-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 80">&#58;&#8202;80&#8202;</span></sup> In the paper,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-22" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-22"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Oppenheimer and Snyder solved Einstein's equations of general relativity for an idealised imploding star, in a model later called the <a href="/wiki/Oppenheimer%E2%80%93Snyder_model" title="Oppenheimer–Snyder model">Oppenheimer–Snyder model</a>, then described the results from far outside the star. The implosion starts as one might expect: the star material rapidly collapses inward. However, as the density of the star increases, <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_time_dilation" title="Gravitational time dilation">gravitational time dilation</a> increases and the collapse, viewed from afar, seems to slow down further and further until the star reaches its Schwarzschild radius, where it appears frozen in time.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 217">&#58;&#8202;217&#8202;</span></sup>
</p><p>In 1958, <a href="/wiki/David_Finkelstein" title="David Finkelstein">David Finkelstein</a> identified the Schwarzschild surface as an <a href="/wiki/Event_horizon" title="Event horizon">event horizon</a>, calling it "a perfect unidirectional membrane: causal influences can cross it in only one direction". This means that events that occur inside the black hole cannot affect events that occur outside the black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-23" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-23"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Finkelstein created a new <a href="/wiki/Kruskal%E2%80%93Szekeres_coordinates" title="Kruskal–Szekeres coordinates">reference frame</a> to include the point of view of infalling observers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bartusiak&#95;21-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bartusiak-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 103">&#58;&#8202;103&#8202;</span></sup> Finkelstein's new frame of reference allowed events at the surface of an imploding star to be related to events far away. By 1962 the two points of view were reconciled, convincing many sceptics that implosion into a black hole made physical sense.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 226">&#58;&#8202;226&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Golden_age">Golden age</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:BH-JPL-A%26A1979.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A black and white image of a black hole with an accretion disk on punch cards. The black hole is visible as a black semicircle in the center with a white ring overlaid. Around it, a bright white accretion disk wraps around the top and bottom of the black hole and to its sides, appearing brightest on the left side of the black hole." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/BH-JPL-A%26A1979.jpg/250px-BH-JPL-A%26A1979.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="124" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/BH-JPL-A%26A1979.jpg/500px-BH-JPL-A%26A1979.jpg 2x" data-file-width="700" data-file-height="346" /></a><figcaption>The first simulated image of a black hole, created by <a href="/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Luminet" title="Jean-Pierre Luminet">Jean-Pierre Luminet</a> in 1978 and featuring the characteristic shadow, <a href="/wiki/Photon_sphere" title="Photon sphere">photon sphere</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_lensing" class="mw-redirect" title="Gravitational lensing">lensed</a> <a href="/wiki/Accretion_disk" title="Accretion disk">accretion disk</a>. The disk is brighter on one side due to <a href="/wiki/Doppler_beaming" class="mw-redirect" title="Doppler beaming">Doppler beaming</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-24" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-25" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-25"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>The era from the mid-1960s to the mid-1970s was the "golden age of black hole research", when general relativity and black holes became mainstream subjects of research.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-26" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-26"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 258">&#58;&#8202;258&#8202;</span></sup>
</p><p>In this period, solutions to the equations of general relativity under various different physical constraints were discovered. In 1963, <a href="/wiki/Roy_Kerr" title="Roy Kerr">Roy Kerr</a> found <a href="/wiki/Kerr_metric" title="Kerr metric">the exact solution</a> for a <a href="/wiki/Rotating_black_hole" title="Rotating black hole">rotating black hole</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Two years later, <a href="/wiki/Ezra_T._Newman" title="Ezra T. Newman">Ezra Newman</a> found the <a href="/wiki/Rotational_symmetry#Rotational_symmetry_with_respect_to_any_angle" title="Rotational symmetry">axisymmetric</a> solution for a black hole that is both rotating and <a href="/wiki/Electrically_charged" class="mw-redirect" title="Electrically charged">electrically charged</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-28" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-28"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 1967, <a href="/wiki/Werner_Israel" title="Werner Israel">Werner Israel</a> found that the Schwarzschild solution was the only possible solution for a nonspinning, uncharged black hole, meaning that a Schwarzschild black hole would be defined by its <a href="/wiki/Mass" title="Mass">mass</a> alone.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-29" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-29"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Similar identities were later found for <a href="/wiki/Reissner%E2%80%93Nordstr%C3%B6m_metric" title="Reissner–Nordström metric">Reissner-Nordstrom</a> and <a href="/wiki/Kerr_metric" title="Kerr metric">Kerr</a> black holes, defined only by their mass and their charge or spin respectively.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-30" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-30"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-31" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-31"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Together, these findings became known as the <a href="/wiki/No-hair_theorem" title="No-hair theorem">no-hair theorem</a>, which states that a stationary black hole is completely described by the three parameters of the <a href="/wiki/Kerr%E2%80%93Newman_metric" title="Kerr–Newman metric">Kerr–Newman metric</a>: mass, angular momentum, and electric charge.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-HeuslerNoHair&#95;32-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HeuslerNoHair-32"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>At first, it was suspected that the strange mathematical singularities found in each of the black hole solutions only appeared due to the assumption that a black hole would be perfectly <a href="/wiki/Rotational_symmetry#Rotational_symmetry_with_respect_to_any_angle" title="Rotational symmetry">spherically symmetric</a>, and therefore the singularities would not appear in generic situations where black holes would not necessarily be symmetric. This view was held in particular by <a href="/wiki/Vladimir_A._Belinsky" class="mw-redirect" title="Vladimir A. Belinsky">Vladimir Belinski</a>, <a href="/wiki/Isaak_Markovich_Khalatnikov" class="mw-redirect" title="Isaak Markovich Khalatnikov">Isaak Khalatnikov</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Evgeny_Lifshitz" title="Evgeny Lifshitz">Evgeny Lifshitz</a>, who tried to prove that no singularities appear in generic solutions, although they would later reverse their positions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-33" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-33"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, in 1965, <a href="/wiki/Roger_Penrose" title="Roger Penrose">Roger Penrose</a> proved that general relativity predicts that singularities appear in all black holes,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-penrose1965&#95;34-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-penrose1965-34"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> although this may not still hold when quantum mechanics is taken into account.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-35" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-35"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Astronomical observations also made great strides during this era. In 1967, <a href="/wiki/Antony_Hewish" title="Antony Hewish">Antony Hewish</a> and <a href="/wiki/Jocelyn_Bell_Burnell" title="Jocelyn Bell Burnell">Jocelyn Bell Burnell</a> discovered <a href="/wiki/Pulsar" title="Pulsar">pulsars</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-36" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-36"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-37" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-37"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and by 1969, these were shown to be rapidly rotating neutron stars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-araa8&#95;265&#95;38-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-araa8_265-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Until that time, neutron stars, like black holes, were regarded as just theoretical curiosities, but the discovery of pulsars showed their physical relevance and spurred a further interest in all types of compact objects that might be formed by gravitational collapse.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-39" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-39"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Based on observations in <a href="/wiki/Royal_Greenwich_Observatory" class="mw-redirect" title="Royal Greenwich Observatory">Greenwich</a> and <a href="/wiki/David_Dunlap_Observatory" title="David Dunlap Observatory">Toronto</a> in the early 1970s, <a href="/wiki/Cygnus_X-1" title="Cygnus X-1">Cygnus X-1</a>, a galactic <a href="/wiki/X-ray" title="X-ray">X-ray</a> source discovered in 1964, became the first astronomical object commonly accepted to be a black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-rolston1997&#95;40-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-rolston1997-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Shipman1975&#95;41-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Shipman1975-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Work by <a href="/wiki/James_Bardeen" class="mw-redirect" title="James Bardeen">James Bardeen</a>, <a href="/wiki/Jacob_Bekenstein" title="Jacob Bekenstein">Jacob Bekenstein</a>, Carter, and Hawking in the early 1970s led to the formulation of <a href="/wiki/Black_hole_thermodynamics" title="Black hole thermodynamics">black hole thermodynamics</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-42" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These laws describe the behaviour of a black hole in a manner analogous to the <a href="/wiki/Laws_of_thermodynamics" title="Laws of thermodynamics">laws of thermodynamics</a>. Under this analogy, the properties of mass, <a href="/wiki/Surface_area" title="Surface area">surface area</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Surface_gravity" title="Surface gravity">surface gravity</a> for a black hole are related to the thermodynamical concepts of energy, <a href="/wiki/Entropy" title="Entropy">entropy</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Temperature" title="Temperature">temperature</a> respectively. The analogy was completed<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Throne-1994&#95;7-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Throne-1994-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 442">&#58;&#8202;442&#8202;</span></sup> when Hawking, in 1974, showed that <a href="/wiki/Quantum_field_theory" title="Quantum field theory">quantum field theory</a> implies that black holes should radiate like a <a href="/wiki/Black_body" title="Black body">black body</a> with a temperature proportional to the surface gravity of the black hole, predicting the effect now known as <a href="/wiki/Hawking_radiation" title="Hawking radiation">Hawking radiation</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Hawking1974&#95;43-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hawking1974-43"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Modern_research_and_observation">Modern research and observation</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:LIGO_measurement_of_gravitational_waves.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Graphs of the first detections of gravitational waves at the Hanford and Livingston LIGO sections, along with comparisons to theoretical predictions, noise, and visual renderings. The readings appear as periodic waves that increase in magnitude over time before suddenly dropping back down." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/LIGO_measurement_of_gravitational_waves.svg/250px-LIGO_measurement_of_gravitational_waves.svg.png" decoding="async" width="190" height="161" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/LIGO_measurement_of_gravitational_waves.svg/500px-LIGO_measurement_of_gravitational_waves.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="499" data-file-height="422" /></a><figcaption>The first detection of gravitational waves, imaged by LIGO observatories in <a href="/wiki/Hanford_Site" title="Hanford Site">Hanford Site</a>, Washington and <a href="/wiki/Livingston,_Louisiana" title="Livingston, Louisiana">Livingston, Louisiana</a></figcaption></figure><p>While Cygnus X-1, a <a href="/wiki/Stellar-mass_black_hole" class="mw-redirect" title="Stellar-mass black hole">stellar-mass black hole</a>, was generally accepted by the scientific community as a black hole by the end of 1973,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-rolston1997&#95;40-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-rolston1997-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> it would be decades before a <a href="/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole" title="Supermassive black hole">supermassive black hole</a> would gain the same broad recognition. Although, as early as the 1960s, physicists such as <a href="/wiki/Donald_Lynden-Bell" title="Donald Lynden-Bell">Donald Lynden-Bell</a> and <a href="/wiki/Martin_Rees" title="Martin Rees">Martin Rees</a> had suggested that powerful <a href="/wiki/Quasars" class="mw-redirect" title="Quasars">quasars</a> in the center of galaxies were powered by <a href="/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics)" title="Accretion (astrophysics)">accreting</a> supermassive black holes, little observational proof existed at the time.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-44" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-44"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-45" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-45"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, the <a href="/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope" title="Hubble Space Telescope">Hubble Space Telescope</a>, launched in the 1990s, found that supermassive black holes were not only present in these <a href="/wiki/Active_galactic_nuclei" class="mw-redirect" title="Active galactic nuclei">active galactic nuclei</a>, but that supermassive black holes in the center of galaxies were ubiquitous: almost every galaxy had a supermassive black hole at its center. The black holes in quiescent galaxies accrete matter more slowly or radiate less efficiently.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ff05&#95;46-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ff05-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-peterson14&#95;47-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-peterson14-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 1999, <a href="/wiki/David_Merritt" title="David Merritt">David Merritt</a> proposed the <a href="/wiki/M%E2%80%93sigma_relation" title="M–sigma relation">M–sigma relation</a>, which related the <a href="/wiki/Dispersion_(statistics)" class="mw-redirect" title="Dispersion (statistics)">dispersion</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Velocity" title="Velocity">velocity</a> of matter in the center <a href="/wiki/Galactic_bulge" title="Galactic bulge">bulge</a> of a galaxy to the mass of the supermassive black hole at its core.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-48" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-48"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Subsequent studies confirmed this correlation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-49" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-49"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>49<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Around the same time, based on telescope observations of the velocities of stars at the center of the Milky Way galaxy, independent work groups led by <a href="/wiki/Andrea_Ghez" class="mw-redirect" title="Andrea Ghez">Andrea Ghez</a> and <a href="/wiki/Reinhard_Genzel" title="Reinhard Genzel">Reinhard Genzel</a> concluded that the compact <a href="/wiki/Astronomical_radio_source" title="Astronomical radio source">radio source</a> in the center of the galaxy, <a href="/wiki/Sagittarius_A*" title="Sagittarius A*">Sagittarius A*</a>, was likely a supermassive black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-50" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-50"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ghez1998&#95;51-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ghez1998-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In late 2015, the <a href="/wiki/LIGO_Scientific_Collaboration" title="LIGO Scientific Collaboration">LIGO Scientific Collaboration</a> and <a href="/wiki/Virgo_interferometer" title="Virgo interferometer">Virgo Collaboration</a> made the <a href="/wiki/First_observation_of_gravitational_waves" title="First observation of gravitational waves">first direct detection</a> of <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_wave" title="Gravitational wave">gravitational waves</a>, named GW150914, representing the first observation of a <a href="/wiki/Black_hole_merger" class="mw-redirect" title="Black hole merger">black hole merger</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-PRL-20160211&#95;52-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-PRL-20160211-52"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At the time of the merger, the black holes were approximately 1.4 billion <a href="/wiki/Light-years" class="mw-redirect" title="Light-years">light-years</a> away from Earth and had masses roughly 30 and 35 times that of the Sun.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ligovirgo16&#95;53-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ligovirgo16-53"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 6">&#58;&#8202;6&#8202;</span></sup> In 2017, <a href="/wiki/Rainer_Weiss" title="Rainer Weiss">Rainer Weiss</a>, <a href="/wiki/Kip_Thorne" title="Kip Thorne">Kip Thorne</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Barry_Barish" title="Barry Barish">Barry Barish</a>, who had spearheaded the project, were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for their work.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-54" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-54"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since the initial discovery in 2015, hundreds more gravitational waves have been observed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-55" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-55"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>55<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Black_hole_-_Messier_87.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Over a black background of empty space, an orange-red donut of gas lies in the center of the image, with a black circle--the black hole&#39;s shadow--in the middle of the donut." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Black_hole_-_Messier_87.jpg/250px-Black_hole_-_Messier_87.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="146" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Black_hole_-_Messier_87.jpg/500px-Black_hole_-_Messier_87.jpg 2x" data-file-width="7416" data-file-height="4320" /></a><figcaption>Image by the <a href="/wiki/Event_Horizon_Telescope" title="Event Horizon Telescope">Event Horizon Telescope</a> of the supermassive black hole in the center of <a href="/wiki/Messier_87" title="Messier 87">Messier 87</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>On 10 April 2019, the first direct image of a black hole and its vicinity was published, following observations made by the <a href="/wiki/Event_Horizon_Telescope" title="Event Horizon Telescope">Event Horizon Telescope</a> (EHT) in 2017 of the supermassive black hole in <a href="/wiki/Messier_87" title="Messier 87">Messier 87</a>'s galactic centre.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-APJL-20190410&#95;56-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-APJL-20190410-56"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2022, the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration released an image of the black hole in the center of the Milky Way galaxy, Sagittarius A*; the data had been collected in 2017.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-eht-press-release&#95;57-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eht-press-release-57"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2020, the <a href="/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics" title="Nobel Prize in Physics">Nobel Prize in Physics</a> was awarded for work on black holes. <a href="/wiki/Andrea_Ghez" class="mw-redirect" title="Andrea Ghez">Andrea Ghez</a> and <a href="/wiki/Reinhard_Genzel" title="Reinhard Genzel">Reinhard Genzel</a> shared one-half for their discovery that Sagittarius A* is a supermassive black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-58" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-58"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>58<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Penrose received the other half for his work showing that the mathematics of general relativity requires the formation of black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-59" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-59"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>59<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Cosmologists lamented that Hawking's extensive theoretical work on black holes would not be honoured since he had died in 2018.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-60" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-60"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Etymology">Etymology</h3></div>
<p>In December 1967, a student reportedly suggested the phrase <i>black hole</i> at a lecture by <a href="/wiki/John_Archibald_Wheeler" title="John Archibald Wheeler">John Wheeler</a>; Wheeler adopted the term for its brevity and "advertising value", and Wheeler's stature in the field ensured it quickly caught on,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bartusiak&#95;21-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bartusiak-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-61" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-61"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> leading some to credit Wheeler with coining the phrase.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-62" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-62"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>62<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, the term was used by others around that time. Science writer Marcia Bartusiak traces the term <i>black hole</i> to physicist <a href="/wiki/Robert_H._Dicke" title="Robert H. Dicke">Robert H. Dicke</a>, who in the early 1960s reportedly compared the phenomenon to the <a href="/wiki/Black_Hole_of_Calcutta" title="Black Hole of Calcutta">Black Hole of Calcutta</a>, notorious as a prison where people entered but never left alive.
The term was used in print by <i><a href="/wiki/Life_(magazine)" title="Life (magazine)">Life</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Science_News" title="Science News">Science News</a></i> magazines in 1963, and by science journalist Ann Ewing in her article <span style="padding-right:.15em;">"</span>'Black Holes' in Space", dated 18 January 1964, which was a report on a meeting of the <a href="/wiki/American_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science" title="American Association for the Advancement of Science">American Association for the Advancement of Science</a> held in Cleveland, Ohio.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bartusiak&#95;21-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bartusiak-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Definition">Definition</h2></div>
<p>A black hole is generally defined as a region of spacetime from which no <a href="/wiki/Randomness#In_the_physical_sciences" title="Randomness">information-carrying</a> signals or objects can escape.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-fz11&#95;63-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-fz11-63"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>63<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, verifying an object as a black hole by this definition would require waiting for an infinite time and at an infinite distance from the black hole to verify that indeed, nothing has escaped, and thus cannot be used to identify a physical black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-booth05&#95;64-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-booth05-64"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There are several other definitions that can be used to describe or identify a black hole, although they are not universally agreed upon by physicists. Among astrophysicists, a black hole is a <a href="/wiki/Compact_object" title="Compact object">compact object</a> with a mass larger than four solar masses.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-curiel19&#95;65-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-curiel19-65"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>65<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A black hole may also be defined as a reservoir of information<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bhwar&#95;66-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bhwar-66"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 142">&#58;&#8202;142&#8202;</span></sup> or a region where space is falling inwards faster than the speed of light.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-67" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-waterfall&#95;68-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-waterfall-68"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>68<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Properties">Properties</h2></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/No-hair_theorem" title="No-hair theorem">no-hair theorem</a> postulates that, once it achieves a stable condition after formation, a black hole has only three independent physical properties: mass, electric charge, and angular momentum; the black hole is otherwise featureless. If the conjecture is true, any two black holes that share the same values for these properties, or parameters, are indistinguishable from one another. The degree to which the conjecture is true is currently an unsolved problem.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-HeuslerNoHair&#95;32-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HeuslerNoHair-32"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-69" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-69"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The simplest static black holes, called <i>Schwarzschild black holes</i>, have mass but neither electric charge nor angular momentum. 
Non-rotating <a href="/wiki/Charged_black_hole" title="Charged black hole">charged black holes</a> are described by the <a href="/wiki/Reissner%E2%80%93Nordstr%C3%B6m_metric" title="Reissner–Nordström metric">Reissner–Nordström metric</a>, while the <a href="/wiki/Kerr_metric" title="Kerr metric">Kerr metric</a> describes a non-charged <a href="/wiki/Rotating_black_hole" title="Rotating black hole">rotating black hole</a>. The most general <a href="/wiki/Stationary_spacetime" title="Stationary spacetime">stationary</a> black hole solution known is the <a href="/wiki/Kerr%E2%80%93Newman_metric" title="Kerr–Newman metric">Kerr–Newman metric</a>, which describes a black hole with both charge and angular momentum.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-shapiro&#95;teukolsky1983&#95;70-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-shapiro_teukolsky1983-70"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Mass">Mass</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_0" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg/250px-seek%3D0-Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="141" data-durationhint="7" data-mwtitle="Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/b/b5/Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg/Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="854" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b5/Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg" type="video/ogg; codecs=&quot;theora&quot;" data-width="1280" data-height="720" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/b/b5/Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg/Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="256" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/b/b5/Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg/Black_Hole_Shadow.ogg.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="426" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Radii for shadow and photon sphere relative to the event horizon</figcaption></figure>
<p>The simplest static black holes have mass but neither electric charge nor angular momentum. Contrary to the popular notion of a black hole "sucking in everything" in its surroundings, from far away, the external gravitational field of a black hole is identical to that of any other body of the same mass.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-71" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-71"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>71<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>While a black hole can theoretically have any positive mass, its charge and angular momentum are limited by its mass, with this limit being greater for more massive black holes. The net electric charge <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle Q}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">

        <mi>Q</mi>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>

  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8752c7023b4b3286800fe3238271bbca681219ed" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.671ex; width:1.838ex; height:2.509ex;" alt="{\displaystyle Q}"></span> and the total angular momentum <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle J}">

for a black hole of mass <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle M}">

      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle M}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f82cade9898ced02fdd08712e5f0c0151758a0dd" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.338ex; width:2.442ex; height:2.176ex;" alt="{\displaystyle M}"></span>, where <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle \epsilon _{0}}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <msub>
          <mi>&#x03F5;<!-- ϵ --></mi>
          <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
            <mn>0</mn>
          </mrow>
        </msub>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle \epsilon _{0}}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d2cae6289b0fe626d1f9472a3416ac73e87bc5a3" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.671ex; width:1.998ex; height:2.009ex;" alt="{\displaystyle \epsilon _{0}}"></span> is the <a href="/wiki/Vacuum_permittivity" title="Vacuum permittivity">vacuum permittivity</a> constant, <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle c}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mi>c</mi>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle c}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/86a67b81c2de995bd608d5b2df50cd8cd7d92455" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.338ex; width:1.007ex; height:1.676ex;" alt="{\displaystyle c}"></span> is the <a href="/wiki/Speed_of_light" title="Speed of light">speed of light</a> and <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle G}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mi>G</mi>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle G}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5f3c8921a3b352de45446a6789b104458c9f90b" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.338ex; width:1.827ex; height:2.176ex;" alt="{\displaystyle G}"></span> is the <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_constant" title="Gravitational constant">gravitational constant</a>. Black holes with the maximum possible combination of charge and spin satisfying this inequality are called <a href="/wiki/Extremal_black_holes" class="mw-redirect" title="Extremal black holes">extremal black holes</a>. Solutions of Einstein's equations that violate this inequality exist, but they do not possess an event horizon. These are so-called <a href="/wiki/Naked_singularity" title="Naked singularity">naked singularities</a> that can be observed from the outside.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-wald&#95;1997&#95;72-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-wald_1997-72"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>72<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Because these singularities make the universe inherently unpredictable, many physicists believe they could not exist.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-73" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-73"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Weak_cosmic_censorship_hypothesis" class="mw-redirect" title="Weak cosmic censorship hypothesis">weak cosmic censorship hypothesis</a>, proposed by Penrose, rules out the formation of such singularities, when they are created through the gravitational collapse of <a href="/wiki/Energy_conditions" class="mw-redirect" title="Energy conditions">realistic matter</a>. However, this theory has not yet been proven, and some physicists believe that naked singularities could exist.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-joshi09&#95;74-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-joshi09-74"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>74<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is also unknown whether black holes could even become extremal, forming naked singularities, since natural processes counteract increasing spin and charge when a black hole becomes near-extremal.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-75" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>75<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The total mass of a black hole can be estimated by analysing the motion of objects near the black hole, such as stars or gas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-peterson14&#95;47-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-peterson14-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Spin_and_angular_momentum">Spin and angular momentum</h3></div>
<p>All black holes spin, often fast—One stellar black hole, <a href="/wiki/GRS_1915%2B105" title="GRS 1915+105">GRS 1915+105</a>, has been estimated to spin at over 1,000 revolutions per second.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-76" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-76"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>76<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Milky Way's central black hole Sagittarius A* rotates at about 90% of the maximum possible rate.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-daly19&#95;77-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-daly19-77"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>77<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-78" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-78"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The spin rate can be inferred from measurements of atomic <a href="/wiki/Spectral_line" title="Spectral line">spectral lines</a> in the X-ray range. As gas near the black hole plunges inward, high energy X-ray emission from electron-positron pairs illuminates the gas further out, appearing red-shifted due to relativistic effects. Depending on the spin of the black hole, this plunge happens at different radii from the hole, with different degrees of redshift. Astronomers can use the gap between the x-ray emission of the outer disk and the redshifted emission from plunging material to determine the spin of the black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-reynolds19&#95;79-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-reynolds19-79"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A newer way to estimate spin is based on the temperature of gases accreting onto the black hole. The method requires an independent measurement of the black hole mass and <a href="/wiki/Orbital_inclination" title="Orbital inclination">inclination angle</a> of the accretion disk followed by computer modelling. Gravitational waves from coalescing binary black holes can also provide the spin of both progenitor black holes and the merged hole, but such events are rare.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-reynolds19&#95;79-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-reynolds19-79"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A spinning black hole has <a href="/wiki/Angular_momentum" title="Angular momentum">angular momentum</a>. The supermassive black hole in the center of the <a href="/wiki/Messier_87" title="Messier 87">Messier 87</a> (M87) galaxy appears to have an angular momentum very close to the maximum theoretical value.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-80" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-80"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>80<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-81" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-81"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>81<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By setting Q equal to 0, the maximum spin of an uncharged black hole can be simplified to<sup id="cite&#95;ref-50SMBH&#95;82-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-50SMBH-82"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
<span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-block"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-display mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle J\leq {\frac {GM^{2}}{c}},}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mi>J</mi>
        <mo>&#x2264;<!-- ≤ --></mo>
        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
          <mfrac>
            <mrow>
              <mi>G</mi>
              <msup>
                <mi>M</mi>
                <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                  <mn>2</mn>

  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f39b611c84e512372f9abae625338cf27d8a4819" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-display mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -1.838ex; width:11.433ex; height:5.676ex;" alt="{\displaystyle J\leq {\frac {GM^{2}}{c}},}"></span>
allowing definition of a <a href="/wiki/Dimensionless" class="mw-redirect" title="Dimensionless">dimensionless</a> spin magnitude such that<sup id="cite&#95;ref-50SMBH&#95;82-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-50SMBH-82"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-83" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-83"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>83<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
<span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-block"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-display mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle 0\leq {\frac {cJ}{GM^{2}}}\leq 1.}">

                <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                  <mn>2</mn>
                </mrow>
              </msup>
            </mrow>
          </mfrac>
        </mrow>

        <mn>1.</mn>
      </mstyle>
