    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle 0\leq {\frac {cJ}{GM^{2}}}\leq 1.}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9316201c21641ceb0f7c92b2bdce82ba680da1f0" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-display mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -2.171ex; width:15.385ex; height:5.509ex;" alt="{\displaystyle 0\leq {\frac {cJ}{GM^{2}}}\leq 1.}"></span>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Charge">Charge</h3></div>
<p>Most black holes are believed to have an approximately neutral charge. For example, Michal Zajaček, Arman Tursunov, Andreas Eckart, and Silke Britzen found the <a href="/wiki/Electric_charge" title="Electric charge">electric charge</a> of Sagittarius A* to be at least ten orders of magnitude below the theoretical maximum.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-zteb18&#95;84-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-zteb18-84"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A charged black hole <a href="/wiki/Coulomb%27s_law" title="Coulomb&#39;s law">repels</a> other like charges just like any other charged object.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-85" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-85"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>85<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> If a black hole were to become charged, particles with an opposite sign of charge would be pulled in by the extra <a href="/wiki/Electromagnetism" title="Electromagnetism">electromagnetic force</a>, while particles with the same sign of charge would be repelled, neutralising the black hole. This effect may not be as strong if the black hole is also spinning.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-86" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-86"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The presence of charge can reduce the diameter of the black hole's shadow by up to 38%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-zteb18&#95;84-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-zteb18-84"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-87" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-87"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>87<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The charge Q for a nonspinning black hole is bounded by
<span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-block"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-display mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle Q\leq {\sqrt {4\pi \epsilon _{0}G}}M,}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mi>Q</mi>
        <mo>&#x2264;<!-- ≤ --></mo>
        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
          <msqrt>
            <mn>4</mn>
            <mi>&#x03C0;<!-- π --></mi>
            <msub>
              <mi>&#x03F5;<!-- ϵ --></mi>
              <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                <mn>0</mn>
              </mrow>
            </msub>
            <mi>G</mi>
          </msqrt>
        </mrow>
        <mi>M</mi>
        <mo>,</mo>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle Q\leq {\sqrt {4\pi \epsilon _{0}G}}M,}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/25fc703fb988c873a29e5ab6df57b468eac9ae75" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-display mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -1.005ex; width:16.669ex; height:3.343ex;" alt="{\displaystyle Q\leq {\sqrt {4\pi \epsilon _{0}G}}M,}"></span>
where G is the gravitational constant and M is the black hole's mass.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-88" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Classification">Classification</h2></div>
<table class="wikitable" style="float:right; margin:0 0 0.5em 1em;">
<caption>Black hole classifications
</caption>
<tbody><tr>
<th>Class</th>
<th>Approx.<br />mass</th>
<th>Approx.<br />radius
</th></tr>
<tr>
<td><a href="/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole#Ultramassive_black_holes" title="Supermassive black hole">Ultramassive black hole</a></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7000900000000000000♠"></span>9</span></sup>–10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7001110000000000000♠"></span>11</span></sup>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">&gt;1,000 <a href="/wiki/Astronomical_unit" title="Astronomical unit">AU</a>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td><a href="/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole" title="Supermassive black hole">Supermassive black hole</a></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7000600000000000000♠"></span>6</span></sup>–10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7000900000000000000♠"></span>9</span></sup>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">0.001–400 <a href="/wiki/Astronomical_unit" title="Astronomical unit">AU</a>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td><a href="/wiki/Intermediate-mass_black_hole" title="Intermediate-mass black hole">Intermediate-mass black hole</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-mc04&#95;89-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mc04-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7000200000000000000♠"></span>2</span></sup>–10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7000500000000000000♠"></span>5</span></sup>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">10<sup>3</sup> km ≈ <a href="/wiki/Earth_radius" title="Earth radius"><i>R</i><sub>Earth</sub></a>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td><a href="/wiki/Stellar_black_hole" title="Stellar black hole">Stellar black hole</a></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">2–150&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">30&#160;km
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td><a href="/wiki/Micro_black_hole" title="Micro black hole">Micro black hole</a></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">up to <i>M</i><sub><a href="/wiki/Moon" title="Moon">Moon</a></sub></td>
<td style="text-align: center;">up to 0.1&#160;mm
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>Black holes are classified by the theory of their formation and by their mass (expressed in terms of <i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>, the mass of the Sun), but these criteria are intertwined. <a href="/wiki/Stellar_black_holes" class="mw-redirect" title="Stellar black holes">Stellar black holes</a> are formed by stellar collapse. The minimum mass of a black hole formed by stellar gravitational collapse is governed by the maximum mass of a neutron star and is believed to be 2-4&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-90" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-90"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>90<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Hypothetical <a href="/wiki/Primordial_black_hole" title="Primordial black hole">primordial black holes</a>, believed to have formed soon after the <a href="/wiki/Big_Bang" title="Big Bang">Big Bang</a>, could be far smaller, with masses as little as <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="6995100000000000000♠"></span>10<sup>−5</sup>&#160;grams</span> at formation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carr&#95;91-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carr-91"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These very small black holes are sometimes called <a href="/wiki/Micro_black_hole" title="Micro black hole">micro black holes</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-92" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-92"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>92<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-93" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-93"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Stellar black holes can have a wide range of masses. Estimates of their maximum mass at formation vary, but generally range from 10-100&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>, with higher estimates for black holes progenated by <a href="/wiki/Metallicity" title="Metallicity">low-metallicity</a> stars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Vink-2021&#95;94-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Vink-2021-94"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Stellar black holes can gain mass via accretion of nearby matter, often from a companion object such as a star<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lph97&#95;95-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lph97-95"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-96" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-96"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> or by merger with another black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-PRL-20160211&#95;52-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-PRL-20160211-52"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Black holes that are larger than stellar black holes but smaller than supermassive black holes are called <a href="/wiki/Intermediate-mass_black_hole" title="Intermediate-mass black hole">intermediate-mass black holes</a>, with approximately <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7002100000000000000♠"></span>10<sup>2</sup></span>-<span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7005100000000000000♠"></span>10<sup>5</sup></span>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>. These black holes seem to be rarer than their stellar and supermassive counterparts, with only a small number of candidates observed so far.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mc04&#95;89-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mc04-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Physicists have speculated that such black holes may form from collisions in <a href="/wiki/Globular_cluster" title="Globular cluster">globular</a> and <a href="/wiki/Star_cluster" title="Star cluster">star</a> clusters or at the center of <a href="/wiki/Dwarf_galaxy" title="Dwarf galaxy">low-mass galaxies</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-97" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-97"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>97<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They may also form as the result of mergers of smaller black holes, with several LIGO observations finding merged black holes within 110–350&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-98" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-98"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>98<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-99" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-99"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>99<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The black holes with the largest masses are called <a href="/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole" title="Supermassive black hole">supermassive black holes</a>, with masses more than <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7006100000000000000♠"></span>10<sup>6</sup></span>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-100" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These black holes are believed to exist at the centers of almost every large galaxy, including the Milky Way.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ff05&#95;46-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ff05-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-peterson14&#95;47-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-peterson14-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some scientists have proposed a subcategory of even larger black holes, called <a href="/wiki/Ultramassive_black_hole" class="mw-redirect" title="Ultramassive black hole">ultramassive black holes</a>, with masses greater than <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7009100000000000000♠"></span>10<sup>9</sup></span>-<span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7010100000000000000♠"></span>10<sup>10</sup></span>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-101" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-101"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>101<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-102" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-102"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Theoretical models predict that the accretion disc that feeds black holes will be unstable once a black hole reaches <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7010500000000000000♠"></span>50<span style="margin-left:0.25em;margin-right:0.15em;">×</span>10<sup>9</sup></span>–<span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7011100000000000000♠"></span>100<span style="margin-left:0.25em;margin-right:0.15em;">×</span>10<sup>9</sup></span>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>, setting a rough upper limit to black hole mass.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-103" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-103"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>103<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-104" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Structure">Structure</h2></div>
<p>While black holes are conceptually invisible sinks of all matter and light, in astronomical settings, their enormous gravity alters the motion of surrounding objects and pulls nearby gas inwards at near-light speed, making the area around black holes the brightest objects in the universe.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-reynolds21&#95;105-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-reynolds21-105"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>105<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="External_geometry">External geometry</h3></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Relativistic_jets">Relativistic jets</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Astrophysical_jet" title="Astrophysical jet">Astrophysical jet</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Black_Hole_Outflows_From_Centaurus_A.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="See caption." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Black_Hole_Outflows_From_Centaurus_A.jpg/250px-Black_Hole_Outflows_From_Centaurus_A.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="253" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Black_Hole_Outflows_From_Centaurus_A.jpg/500px-Black_Hole_Outflows_From_Centaurus_A.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2669" data-file-height="2702" /></a><figcaption>Relativistic jets from the supermassive black hole in <a href="/wiki/Centaurus_A" title="Centaurus A">Centaurus A</a> extend <a href="/wiki/Perpendicular" title="Perpendicular">perpendicularly</a> from the galaxy.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Some black holes have relativistic jets—thin streams of <a href="/wiki/Plasma_(physics)" title="Plasma (physics)">plasma</a> travelling away from the black hole at more than one-tenth of the speed of light.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mr99&#95;106-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mr99-106"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>106<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A small fraction of the matter falling towards the black hole gets accelerated away along the hole rotation axis.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-107" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-107"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>107<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These jets can extend as far as millions of <a href="/wiki/Light-year" title="Light-year">light-years</a> from the black hole itself.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-108" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-108"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>108<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Black holes of any mass can have jets.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nemmen12&#95;109-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nemmen12-109"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, they are typically observed around spinning black holes with strongly-magnetized accretion disks.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bmr18&#95;110-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bmr18-110"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>110<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-111" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-111"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Relativistic jets were more common in the <a href="/wiki/Chronology_of_the_universe#Gravity_builds_cosmic_structure" title="Chronology of the universe">early universe</a>, when galaxies and their corresponding supermassive black holes were rapidly gaining mass.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bmr18&#95;110-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bmr18-110"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>110<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-112" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-112"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>112<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> All black holes with jets also have an accretion disk, but the jets are usually brighter than the disk.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mr99&#95;106-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mr99-106"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>106<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-113" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-113"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>113<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <i><a href="/wiki/Quasar" title="Quasar">Quasars</a>,</i> typically found in other galaxies, are believed to be supermassive black holes with jets; <i><a href="/wiki/Microquasar" title="Microquasar">microquasars</a></i> are believed to be stellar-mass objects with jets, typically observed in the Milky Way.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-114" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-114"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>114<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The mechanism of formation of jets is not yet known,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nemmen12&#95;109-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nemmen12-109"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but several options have been proposed. One method proposed to fuel these jets is the <a href="/wiki/Blandford-Znajek_process" class="mw-redirect" title="Blandford-Znajek process">Blandford-Znajek process</a>, which suggests that the dragging of <a href="/wiki/Magnetic_field_lines" class="mw-redirect" title="Magnetic field lines">magnetic field lines</a> by a black hole's rotation could launch jets of matter into space.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lwb00&#95;115-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lwb00-115"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>115<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-116" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-116"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>116<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Penrose_process" title="Penrose process">Penrose process</a>, which involves extraction of a black hole's <a href="/wiki/Rotational_energy" title="Rotational energy">rotational energy</a>, has also been proposed as a potential mechanism of jet propulsion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-117" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-117"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-118" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-118"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>118<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Accretion_disk">Accretion disk</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Accretion_disk" title="Accretion disk">Accretion disk</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Black_Hole_Desktop_%26_Phone_Wallpapers_(SVS14146_-_BH_accretion_disk_viz_desktop).png" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="An black hole with an orange-red accretion disk. The disk is wrapped around the black hole in a ring across its center and semicircles around the top and bottom of the black hole. The semicircles are actually the part of the accretion disk that is behind the black hole, and appears gravitationally lensed to be above and below the hole." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Black_Hole_Desktop_%26_Phone_Wallpapers_%28SVS14146_-_BH_accretion_disk_viz_desktop%29.png/250px-Black_Hole_Desktop_%26_Phone_Wallpapers_%28SVS14146_-_BH_accretion_disk_viz_desktop%29.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Black_Hole_Desktop_%26_Phone_Wallpapers_%28SVS14146_-_BH_accretion_disk_viz_desktop%29.png/500px-Black_Hole_Desktop_%26_Phone_Wallpapers_%28SVS14146_-_BH_accretion_disk_viz_desktop%29.png 2x" data-file-width="1920" data-file-height="1080" /></a><figcaption>Visualization of a black hole with an orange accretion disk. The parts of the disk circling over and under the hole are actually gravitationally lensed from the back side of the black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-119" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-119"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>119<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-120" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-120"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>120<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Due to <a href="/wiki/Conservation_of_angular_momentum" class="mw-redirect" title="Conservation of angular momentum">conservation of angular momentum</a>, gas falling into the <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_well" class="mw-redirect" title="Gravitational well">gravitational well</a> created by a massive object will typically form a disk-like structure around the object.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-121" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-121"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>121<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 242">&#58;&#8202;242&#8202;</span></sup> As the disk's angular momentum is transferred outward due to internal processes, its matter falls farther inward, converting its gravitational energy into heat and releasing a large amount of x-rays.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-122" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-122"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-pt74&#95;123-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pt74-123"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>123<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The temperature of these disks can range from thousands to millions of <a href="/wiki/Kelvin" title="Kelvin">kelvins</a>, and temperatures differ throughout a single accretion disk.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-124" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-124"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>124<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-125" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-125"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>125<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Accretion disks can also emit in other parts of the <a href="/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum" title="Electromagnetic spectrum">electromagnetic spectrum</a>, depending on the disk's <a href="/wiki/Turbulence" title="Turbulence">turbulence</a> and <a href="/wiki/Magnetisation" class="mw-redirect" title="Magnetisation">magnetisation</a> and the black hole's mass and angular momentum.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-126" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-126"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Accretion disks can be defined as geometrically thin or geometrically thick. Geometrically thin disks are mostly confined to the black hole's equatorial plane and have a well-defined edge at the <a href="/wiki/Innermost_stable_circular_orbit" title="Innermost stable circular orbit">innermost stable circular orbit</a> (ISCO), while geometrically thick disks are supported by internal pressure and temperature and can extend inside the ISCO. Disks with high rates of <a href="/wiki/Electron_scattering" title="Electron scattering">electron scattering</a> and absorption, appearing bright and <a href="/wiki/Opaque" class="mw-redirect" title="Opaque">opaque</a>, are called <i>optically thick</i>; <i>optically thin</i> disks are more <a href="/wiki/Translucent" class="mw-redirect" title="Translucent">translucent</a> and produce fainter images when viewed from afar.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-wang25&#95;127-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-wang25-127"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>127<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Accretion disks of black holes accreting beyond the <a href="/wiki/Eddington_limit" class="mw-redirect" title="Eddington limit">Eddington limit</a> are often referred to as <i>polish donuts</i> due to their thick, <a href="/wiki/Toroid" title="Toroid">toroidal</a> shape that resembles that of a <a href="/wiki/Donut" class="mw-redirect" title="Donut">donut</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-128" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-128"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>128<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-129" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-129"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>129<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Quasar accretion disks are expected to usually appear blue in colour.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-130" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-130"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The disk for a stellar black hole, on the other hand, would likely look orange, yellow, or red, with its inner regions being the brightest.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-131" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-131"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>131<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Theoretical research suggests that the hotter a disk is, the bluer it should be, although this is not always supported by observations of real astronomical objects.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-132" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-132"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>132<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Accretion disk colours may also be altered by the <a href="/wiki/Doppler_effect" title="Doppler effect">Doppler effect</a>, with the part of the disk travelling towards an observer appearing bluer and brighter and the part of the disk travelling away from the observer appearing redder and dimmer.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jtft15&#95;133-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jtft15-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-134" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-134"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>134<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Innermost_stable_circular_orbit_(ISCO)"><span id="Innermost_stable_circular_orbit_.28ISCO.29"></span>Innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO)</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Innermost_stable_circular_orbit" title="Innermost stable circular orbit">Innermost stable circular orbit</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:How_to_Measure_the_Spin_of_a_Black_Hole.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Artistic depiction of three black holes, showing that the accretion disk is closer to the black hole if it is orbiting in the same direction that the black hole is rotating. Graphs next to these images show changes in the x-ray spectra released by the disks." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/How_to_Measure_the_Spin_of_a_Black_Hole.jpg/250px-How_to_Measure_the_Spin_of_a_Black_Hole.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="188" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/How_to_Measure_the_Spin_of_a_Black_Hole.jpg/500px-How_to_Measure_the_Spin_of_a_Black_Hole.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3426" data-file-height="2570" /></a><figcaption>Since particles in a black hole's accretion disk must orbit at or outside the ISCO, astronomers can observe the properties of accretion disks to determine black hole spins.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-135" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-135"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>135<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>In <a href="/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation" title="Newton&#39;s law of universal gravitation">Newtonian gravity</a>, <a href="/wiki/Test_particle" title="Test particle">test particles</a> can stably orbit at arbitrary distances from a central object. In general relativity, however, there exists a smallest possible radius for which a massive particle can orbit stably. Any infinitesimal inward <a href="/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy)" title="Perturbation (astronomy)">perturbations</a> to this orbit will lead to the particle <a href="/wiki/Orbital_decay" title="Orbital decay">spiraling into</a> the black hole, and any outward perturbations will, depending on the energy, cause the particle to spiral in, move to a stable orbit further from the black hole, or escape to infinity. This orbit is called the <b>innermost stable circular orbit</b>, or ISCO.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Misner-1973&#95;136-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Misner-1973-136"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>136<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-jtb15&#95;137-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jtb15-137"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The location of the ISCO depends on the spin of the black hole and the <a href="/wiki/Spin_(physics)" title="Spin (physics)">spin</a> of the particle itself. In the case of a Schwarzschild black hole (spin zero) and a particle without spin, the location of the ISCO is:
<span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-block"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-display mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle r_{\rm {ISCO}}=3\,r_{\text{s}}={\frac {6\,GM}{c^{2}}},}">

            <mtext>s</mtext>
          </mrow>
        </msub>

        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">

              <mn>6</mn>
              <mspace width="thinmathspace" />

              <mi>c</mi>
              <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                <mn>2</mn>

            </msup>

</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5dc67a392f63faf10fdb2eee39b5f382dbb5a936" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.671ex; width:1.929ex; height:2.009ex;" alt="{\displaystyle r_{\text{s}}}"></span> is the Schwarzschild radius of the black hole, <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle G}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mi>G</mi>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle G}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5f3c8921a3b352de45446a6789b104458c9f90b" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.338ex; width:1.827ex; height:2.176ex;" alt="{\displaystyle G}"></span> is the gravitational constant, and <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle c}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mi>c</mi>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle c}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/86a67b81c2de995bd608d5b2df50cd8cd7d92455" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.338ex; width:1.007ex; height:1.676ex;" alt="{\displaystyle c}"></span> is the speed of light.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bardeen1972&#95;138-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bardeen1972-138"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
The radius of this orbit changes slightly based on particle spin.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-zwgsl18&#95;139-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-zwgsl18-139"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>139<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-140" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-140"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>140<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For charged black holes, the ISCO moves inwards.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-zwgsl18&#95;139-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-zwgsl18-139"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>139<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For spinning black holes, the ISCO is moved inwards for particles orbiting in the same direction that the black hole is spinning (<a href="/wiki/Retrograde_and_prograde_motion" title="Retrograde and prograde motion">prograde</a>) and outwards for particles orbiting in the opposite direction (retrograde).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jtb15&#95;137-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jtb15-137"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For example, the ISCO for a particle orbiting retrograde can be as far out as about <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle 4.5r_{\text{s}}}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mn>4.5</mn>
        <msub>
          <mi>r</mi>
          <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
            <mtext>s</mtext>
          </mrow>
        </msub>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle 4.5r_{\text{s}}}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/93238fa9219c830b64b8c31fd03992777525b801" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.671ex; width:4.901ex; height:2.509ex;" alt="{\displaystyle 4.5r_{\text{s}}}"></span>, while the ISCO for a particle orbiting prograde can be as close as at the event horizon itself.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jtb15&#95;137-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jtb15-137"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-141" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-141"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>141<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Photon_sphere_and_shadow">Photon sphere and shadow</h4></div>
<p><span class="anchor" id="Photon&#95;sphere"></span>
</p>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Photon_sphere" title="Photon sphere">Photon sphere</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_1" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg/250px-seek%3D6-Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="250" data-durationhint="8" data-mwtitle="Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/63/Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg" type="video/ogg; codecs=&quot;theora&quot;" data-width="430" data-height="430" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/6/63/Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg/Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="144" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/6/63/Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg/Photon_capture_by_a_Schwarzschild_black_hole_-_video.ogg.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="240" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Video of a photon being captured by a Schwarzschild black hole </figcaption></figure>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Photon_sphere" title="Photon sphere">photon sphere</a> is a spherical boundary for which photons moving on tangents to that sphere are bent completely around the black hole, possibly orbiting multiple times.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-142" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-142"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>142<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For Schwarzschild black holes, the photon sphere has a radius 1.5 times the Schwarzschild radius.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ll19&#95;143-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ll19-143"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>143<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-qiao22&#95;144-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-qiao22-144"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>144<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> When viewed from a great distance, the photon sphere creates an observable <b>black hole shadow</b>.<span class="anchor" id="Black&#95;hole&#95;shadow"></span><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ll19&#95;143-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ll19-143"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>143<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since no light emerges from within the black hole, this shadow is the limit for possible observations.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-145" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-145"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>145<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 152">&#58;&#8202;152&#8202;</span></sup> The shadow of colliding black holes should have characteristic warped shapes, allowing scientists to detect black holes that are about to merge.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-prd84&#95;6&#95;146-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-prd84_6-146"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>While light can still escape from the photon sphere, any light that crosses the photon sphere on an inbound trajectory will be captured by the black hole. Therefore, any light that reaches an outside observer from the photon sphere must have been emitted by objects between the photon sphere and the event horizon.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-prd84&#95;6&#95;146-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-prd84_6-146"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Light emitted towards the photon sphere may also curve around the black hole and return to the emitter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-147" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>For a rotating, uncharged black hole, the radius of the photon sphere depends on the spin parameter and whether the photon is orbiting prograde or retrograde.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bardeen1972&#95;138-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bardeen1972-138"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For a photon orbiting prograde, the photon sphere will be 0.5-1.5 Schwarzschild radii from the center of the black hole, while for a photon orbiting retrograde, the photon sphere will be between 3-4 Schwarzschild radii from the center of the black hole. The exact locations of the photon spheres depend on the <a href="/wiki/Magnitude_(mathematics)#numbers" title="Magnitude (mathematics)">magnitude</a> of the black hole's rotation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-148" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-148"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For a charged, nonrotating black hole, there will only be one photon sphere, and the radius of the photon sphere will decrease for increasing black hole charge.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-149" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-149"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>149<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For non-<a href="/wiki/Extremal_black_hole" title="Extremal black hole">extremal</a>, charged, rotating black holes, there will always be two photon spheres, with the exact radii depending on the parameters of the black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-150" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-150"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>150<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Ergosphere">Ergosphere</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Ergosphere" title="Ergosphere">Ergosphere</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Ergosphere_and_event_horizon_of_a_rotating_black_hole_(no_animation).gif" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="The ergosphere as a peanut-shaped region that touches the event horizon in the middle and then bulges outwards at the poles. The black hole depicted has a spin 99% of the maximum." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Ergosphere_and_event_horizon_of_a_rotating_black_hole_%28no_animation%29.gif/250px-Ergosphere_and_event_horizon_of_a_rotating_black_hole_%28no_animation%29.gif" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/00/Ergosphere_and_event_horizon_of_a_rotating_black_hole_%28no_animation%29.gif 2x" data-file-width="494" data-file-height="494" /></a><figcaption>The ergosphere is a region outside of the event horizon, where objects cannot remain in place.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-viss&#95;151-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-viss-151"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>151<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Near a rotating black hole, spacetime rotates similar to a vortex. The rotating spacetime will drag any matter and light into rotation around the spinning black hole. This effect of general relativity, called <a href="/wiki/Frame_dragging" class="mw-redirect" title="Frame dragging">frame dragging</a>, gets stronger closer to the spinning mass. The region of spacetime in which it is impossible to stay still is called the ergosphere.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-reynolds18&#95;152-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-reynolds18-152"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>152<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The ergosphere of a black hole is a volume bounded by the black hole's event horizon and the <i>ergosurface</i>, which coincides with the event horizon at the poles but bulges out from it around the equator.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-viss&#95;151-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-viss-151"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>151<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Matter and radiation can escape from the ergosphere. Through the <a href="/wiki/Penrose_process" title="Penrose process">Penrose process</a>, objects can emerge from the ergosphere with more energy than they entered with. The extra energy is taken from the rotational energy of the black hole, slowing down the rotation of the black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2019&#95;153-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2019-153"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>153<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 268">&#58;&#8202;268&#8202;</span></sup> A variation of the Penrose process in the presence of strong magnetic fields, the <a href="/wiki/Blandford%E2%80%93Znajek_process" title="Blandford–Znajek process">Blandford–Znajek process</a>, is considered a likely mechanism for the enormous luminosity and relativistic jets of <a href="/wiki/Quasars" class="mw-redirect" title="Quasars">quasars</a> and other <a href="/wiki/Active_galactic_nuclei" class="mw-redirect" title="Active galactic nuclei">active galactic nuclei</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lwb00&#95;115-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lwb00-115"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>115<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-154" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-154"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>154<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Plunging_region">Plunging region</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Plunging_region" title="Plunging region">Plunging region</a></div>
<p>The observable region of spacetime around a black hole closest to its event horizon is called the plunging region. In this area it is no longer possible for free falling matter to follow circular orbits or stop a final descent into the black hole. Instead, it will rapidly plunge toward the black hole at close to the speed of light, growing increasingly hot and producing a characteristic, detectable <a href="/wiki/Thermal_radiation" title="Thermal radiation">thermal emission</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-155" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-155"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>155<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-156" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-156"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, light and radiation emitted from this region can still escape from the black hole's gravitational pull.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-prisco&#95;157-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-prisco-157"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>157<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Radius">Radius</h3></div>
<p>For a nonspinning, uncharged black hole, the radius of the event horizon, or Schwarzschild radius, is proportional to the mass, <i>M</i>, through
<span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-block"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-display mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle r_{\mathrm {s} }={\frac {2GM}{c^{2}}}\approx 2.95\,{\frac {M}{M_{\odot }}}~\mathrm {km,} }">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <msub>
          <mi>r</mi>
          <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
            <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
              <mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi>
            </mrow>
          </mrow>
        </msub>

        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
          <mfrac>
            <mrow>
              <mn>2</mn>
              <mi>G</mi>
              <mi>M</mi>
            </mrow>
            <msup>
              <mi>c</mi>
              <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                <mn>2</mn>
              </mrow>
            </msup>
          </mfrac>
        </mrow>
        <mo>&#x2248;<!-- ≈ --></mo>
        <mn>2.95</mn>
        <mspace width="thinmathspace" />
        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
          <mfrac>
            <mi>M</mi>
            <msub>
              <mi>M</mi>
              <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                <mo>&#x2299;<!-- ⊙ --></mo>
              </mrow>
            </msub>
          </mfrac>
        </mrow>
        <mtext>&#xA0;</mtext>
        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
          <mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi>
          <mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi>
          <mo>,</mo>
        </mrow>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle r_{\mathrm {s} }={\frac {2GM}{c^{2}}}\approx 2.95\,{\frac {M}{M_{\odot }}}~\mathrm {km,} }</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f49ba5dfc447bc1aad8107669b07ab45532026e2" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-display mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -2.338ex; width:27.906ex; height:5.843ex;" alt="{\displaystyle r_{\mathrm {s} }={\frac {2GM}{c^{2}}}\approx 2.95\,{\frac {M}{M_{\odot }}}~\mathrm {km,} }"></span>
where <i>r</i><sub>s</sub> is the Schwarzschild radius, <i>G</i> is the <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_constant" title="Gravitational constant">gravitational constant</a>, <i>c</i> is the <a href="/wiki/Speed_of_light" title="Speed of light">speed of light</a>, and <i>M</i><sub>☉</sub> is the <a href="/wiki/Solar_mass" title="Solar mass">mass of the Sun</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-158" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-158"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>158<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 124">&#58;&#8202;124&#8202;</span></sup> For a black hole of the same mass with nonzero spin or electric charge, the radius is smaller.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-159" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-159"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>Note 1<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As a black hole's charge and spin approach the maximum allowed value, the radius of the event horizon nears
<span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-block"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-display mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math display="block" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle r_{\mathrm {+} }={\frac {GM}{c^{2}}},}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <msub>
          <mi>r</mi>
          <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
            <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
              <mo>+</mo>
            </mrow>
          </mrow>
        </msub>
        <mo>=</mo>
        <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
          <mfrac>
            <mrow>
              <mi>G</mi>
              <mi>M</mi>
            </mrow>
            <msup>
              <mi>c</mi>
              <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
                <mn>2</mn>
              </mrow>
            </msup>
          </mfrac>
        </mrow>
        <mo>,</mo>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle r_{\mathrm {+} }={\frac {GM}{c^{2}}},}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8e79ba5697a5411721767bec3a87a34640a75ef8" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-display mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -2.171ex; width:11.41ex; height:5.676ex;" alt="{\displaystyle r_{\mathrm {+} }={\frac {GM}{c^{2}}},}"></span>
half the radius of a nonspinning, uncharged black hole of the same mass.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-160" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-160"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>159<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Since the volume within the Schwarzschild radius increases with the cube of the radius, average density of a black hole inside its Schwarzschild radius is inversely proportional to the square of its mass: supermassive black holes are much less dense than stellar black holes. The average density of a 10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7000800000000000000♠"></span>8</span></sup>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub> black hole is comparable to that of water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CMS1999&#95;161-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CMS1999-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-162" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-162"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Event_horizon">Event horizon</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Event_horizon" title="Event horizon">Event horizon</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:304px;max-width:304px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:302px;max-width:302px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:BH-no-escape-1.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A light cone near the event horizon of a black hole with arrows coming out of it to show the directions that a particle there can move. It is far enough away from the event horizon that that it is not warped towards the event horizon and a particle there can move in any direction." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/BH-no-escape-1.svg/330px-BH-no-escape-1.svg.png" decoding="async" width="300" height="75" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/BH-no-escape-1.svg/960px-BH-no-escape-1.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1333" data-file-height="333" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Far away from the black hole, a particle can move in any direction, as illustrated by the set of arrows. It is restricted only by the speed of light.</div></div></div><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:302px;max-width:302px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:BH-no-escape-2.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A light cone very close to the event horizon of a black hole, with arrows coming out of it to show the directions that a particle there can move. It is bent towards the event horizon, with more arrows going towards the event horizon than away, but a particle can still travel away from the event horizon." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/BH-no-escape-2.svg/330px-BH-no-escape-2.svg.png" decoding="async" width="300" height="75" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/BH-no-escape-2.svg/960px-BH-no-escape-2.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1333" data-file-height="333" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Closer to the black hole, spacetime starts to deform. There are more paths going towards the black hole than paths moving away.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-163" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-163"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>Note 2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:302px;max-width:302px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:BH-no-escape-3.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A light cone inside of the event horizon of a black hole, with arrows coming out of it to show the directions that a particle there can move. All of the arrows lead further inside the event horizon, with none leading outwards." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/BH-no-escape-3.svg/330px-BH-no-escape-3.svg.png" decoding="async" width="300" height="75" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/BH-no-escape-3.svg/960px-BH-no-escape-3.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1333" data-file-height="333" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Inside of the event horizon, all paths bring the particle closer to the centre of the black hole. It is no longer possible for the particle to escape.</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The defining feature of a black hole is the existence of an event horizon, a boundary in <a href="/wiki/Spacetime" title="Spacetime">spacetime</a> through which matter and light can pass only inward towards the center of the black hole. Nothing, not even light, can escape from inside the event horizon.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-164" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-164"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>162<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-165" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-165"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The event horizon is referred to as such because if an event occurs within the boundary, information from that event cannot reach or affect an outside observer, making it impossible to determine whether such an event occurred.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-JCWheeler-2007&#95;166-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JCWheeler-2007-166"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 179">&#58;&#8202;179&#8202;</span></sup>
For non-rotating black holes, the geometry of the event horizon is precisely spherical, while for rotating black holes, the event horizon is <a href="/wiki/Oblate_spheroid" class="mw-redirect" title="Oblate spheroid">oblate</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Smarr1973&#95;167-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Smarr1973-167"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>165<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Wiltshire2009&#95;168-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Wiltshire2009-168"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>166<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>To a distant observer, a clock near a black hole would appear to tick more slowly than one further from the black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2004&#95;169-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2004-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 217">&#58;&#8202;217&#8202;</span></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-scienceofinterstellar&#95;170-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-scienceofinterstellar-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This effect, known as <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_time_dilation" title="Gravitational time dilation">gravitational time dilation</a>, would also cause an object falling into a black hole to appear to slow as it approached the event horizon, never quite reaching the horizon from the perspective of an outside observer.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2004&#95;169-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2004-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 218">&#58;&#8202;218&#8202;</span></sup> All processes on this object would appear to slow down, and any light emitted by the object to appear redder and dimmer, an effect known as <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_redshift" title="Gravitational redshift">gravitational redshift</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-171" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-171"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>169<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> An object falling from half of a Schwarzschild radius above the event horizon would fade away until it could no longer be seen, disappearing from view within one hundredth of a second.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-172" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>170<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It would also appear to flatten onto the black hole, joining all other material that had ever fallen into the hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-173" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-173"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>171<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>On the other hand, an observer falling into a black hole would not notice any of these effects as they cross the event horizon. Their own clocks appear to them to tick normally, and they cross the event horizon after a finite time without noting any singular behaviour. In <a href="/wiki/General_relativity" title="General relativity">general relativity</a>, it is impossible to determine the location of the event horizon from local observations, due to Einstein's <a href="/wiki/Equivalence_principle" title="Equivalence principle">equivalence principle</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2004&#95;169-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2004-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 222">&#58;&#8202;222&#8202;</span></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-HamiltonA&#95;174-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HamiltonA-174"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>172<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Internal_geometry">Internal geometry</h3></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Cauchy_horizon">Cauchy horizon</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Cauchy_horizon" title="Cauchy horizon">Cauchy horizon</a></div>
<p>Black holes that are rotating and/or charged have an inner horizon, often called the Cauchy horizon, inside of the black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pi90&#95;175-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pi90-175"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The inner horizon is divided up into two segments: an ingoing section and an outgoing section.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-st14&#95;176-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-st14-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>At the ingoing section of the Cauchy horizon, radiation and matter that fall into the black hole would build up at the horizon, causing the curvature of spacetime to go to infinity. This would cause an observer falling in to experience tidal forces.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pi90&#95;175-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pi90-175"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-st14&#95;176-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-st14-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This phenomenon is often called <a href="/wiki/Mass_inflation" title="Mass inflation">mass inflation</a>, since it is associated with a <a href="/wiki/Parameter" title="Parameter">parameter</a> dictating the black hole's internal mass <a href="/wiki/Exponential_growth" title="Exponential growth">growing exponentially</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pi90&#95;175-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pi90-175"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-mo12&#95;177-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mo12-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the buildup of tidal forces is called the mass-inflation singularity<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ori91&#95;178-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ori91-178"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>176<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-st14&#95;176-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-st14-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> or Cauchy horizon singularity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-179" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-179"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>177<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-180" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-180"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>178<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some physicists have argued that in realistic black holes, accretion and Hawking radiation would stop mass inflation from occurring.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-181" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-181"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>179<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-182" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-182"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>180<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>At the outgoing section of the inner horizon, infalling radiation would <a href="/wiki/Backscatter" title="Backscatter">backscatter</a> off of the black hole's spacetime curvature and travel outward, building up at the outgoing Cauchy horizon. This would cause an infalling observer to experience a gravitational <a href="/wiki/Shock_wave" title="Shock wave">shock wave</a> and tidal forces as the spacetime curvature at the horizon grew to infinity. This buildup of tidal forces is called the <a href="/wiki/Shock_singularity" title="Shock singularity">shock singularity</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mo12&#95;177-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mo12-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-st14&#95;176-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-st14-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Both of these singularities are <a href="/wiki/Penrose-Hawking_singularity_theorems#Singularities" class="mw-redirect" title="Penrose-Hawking singularity theorems">weak</a>, meaning that an object crossing them would only be deformed a finite amount by tidal forces, even though the spacetime curvature would still be infinite at the singularity. This is as opposed to a <a href="/wiki/Penrose-Hawking_singularity_theorems#Singularities" class="mw-redirect" title="Penrose-Hawking singularity theorems">strong singularity</a>, where an object hitting the singularity would be stretched and squeezed by an infinite amount.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ori91&#95;178-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ori91-178"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>176<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-mo12&#95;177-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mo12-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They are also <a href="/wiki/Penrose-Hawking_singularity_theorems#Singularities" class="mw-redirect" title="Penrose-Hawking singularity theorems">null singularities</a>, meaning that a photon could travel <a href="/wiki/Parallel_(geometry)" title="Parallel (geometry)">parallel</a> to them without ever being intercepted.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-st14&#95;176-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-st14-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Singularity">Singularity</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_singularity" title="Gravitational singularity">Gravitational singularity</a></div>
<p>Ignoring quantum effects, every black hole has a singularity inside, points where the curvature of spacetime becomes infinite, and <a href="/wiki/Geodesic" title="Geodesic">geodesics</a> terminate within a finite <a href="/wiki/Proper_time" title="Proper time">proper time</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2004&#95;169-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2004-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 205">&#58;&#8202;205&#8202;</span></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-hp70&#95;183-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hp70-183"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>181<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For a non-rotating black hole, this region takes the shape of a single point; for a rotating black hole it is smeared out to form a <a href="/wiki/Ring_singularity" title="Ring singularity">ring singularity</a> that lies in the plane of rotation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2004&#95;169-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2004-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 264">&#58;&#8202;264&#8202;</span></sup> In both cases, the singular region has zero volume. All of the mass of the black hole ends up in the singularity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2004&#95;169-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2004-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 252">&#58;&#8202;252&#8202;</span></sup> Since the singularity has nonzero mass in an infinitely small space, it can be thought of as having infinite <a href="/wiki/Mass_density" class="mw-redirect" title="Mass density">density</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-184" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-184"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_2" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg/250px-seek%3D0-BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="270" data-durationhint="6" data-mwtitle="BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/91/BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg" type="video/ogg; codecs=&quot;theora&quot;" data-width="380" data-height="410" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/9/91/BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg/BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="134" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/9/91/BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg/BKL_dynamics_-_video.ogg.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="222" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Chaotic oscillations of spacetime experienced by an object approaching a gravitational singularity</figcaption></figure>
<p>Observers falling into a Schwarzschild black hole (i.e., non-rotating and not charged) cannot avoid being carried into the singularity once they cross the event horizon.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-185" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-185"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>183<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-186" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-186"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>184<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As they fall further into the black hole, they will be torn apart by the growing <a href="/wiki/Tidal_force" title="Tidal force">tidal forces</a> in a process sometimes referred to as <a href="/wiki/Spaghettification" title="Spaghettification">spaghettification</a> or the <i>noodle effect</i>. Eventually, they will reach the singularity and be crushed into an infinitely small point.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-JCWheeler-2007&#95;166-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JCWheeler-2007-166"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 182">&#58;&#8202;182&#8202;</span></sup> However, any perturbations, such as those caused by matter or radiation falling in, would cause space to <a href="/wiki/BKL_singularity" title="BKL singularity">oscillate chaotically</a> near the singularity. Any matter falling in would experience intense tidal forces rapidly changing in direction, all while being compressed into an increasingly small volume.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-187" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-187"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>185<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-scienceofinterstellar&#95;170-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-scienceofinterstellar-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 231">&#58;&#8202;231&#8202;</span></sup>
</p><p>Alternative forms of general relativity, including addition of some quantum effects, can lead to <i>regular</i>, or <i>nonsingular</i>, black holes without singularities.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-188" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-188"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>186<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-189" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-189"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For example, the <a href="/wiki/Fuzzball_(string_theory)" title="Fuzzball (string theory)">fuzzball</a> model, based on <a href="/wiki/String_theory" title="String theory">string theory</a>, states that black holes are actually made up of <a href="/wiki/Quantum_state" title="Quantum state">quantum microstates</a> and need not have a singularity or an event horizon.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-190" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-190"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>188<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-191" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-191"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>189<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The theory of <a href="/wiki/Loop_quantum_gravity" title="Loop quantum gravity">loop quantum gravity</a> proposes that the curvature and density at the center of a black hole is large, but not infinite.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-192" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-192"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>190<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Formation">Formation</h2></div>
<p>Black holes are formed by <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_collapse" title="Gravitational collapse">gravitational collapse</a> of massive stars, either by direct collapse or during a supernova explosion in a process called <i>fallback</i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-193" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-193"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>191<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Black holes can result from the merger of two <a href="/wiki/Neutron_star" title="Neutron star">neutron stars</a> or a neutron star and a black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-194" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-194"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>192<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Other more speculative mechanisms include <a href="/wiki/Primordial_black_hole" title="Primordial black hole">primordial black holes</a> created from density fluctuations in the early universe, the collapse of <a href="/wiki/Dark_star_(Newtonian_mechanics)" title="Dark star (Newtonian mechanics)">dark stars</a>, a hypothetical object powered by annihilation of <a href="/wiki/Dark_matter" title="Dark matter">dark matter</a>, or from hypothetical <a href="/wiki/Self-interacting_dark_matter" title="Self-interacting dark matter">self-interacting dark matter</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-195" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-195"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>193<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Supernova">Supernova</h3></div>
<p>Gravitational collapse occurs when an object's internal <a href="/wiki/Pressure" title="Pressure">pressure</a> is insufficient to resist the object's own gravity. At the end of a star's life, it will run out of <a href="/wiki/Hydrogen" title="Hydrogen">hydrogen</a> to <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_fusion" title="Nuclear fusion">fuse</a>, and will start fusing more and more massive elements, until it gets to <a href="/wiki/Iron" title="Iron">iron</a>. Since the fusion of elements heavier than iron would <a href="/wiki/Endothermic_reaction" class="mw-redirect" title="Endothermic reaction">require more energy than it would release</a>, nuclear fusion ceases. If the iron core of the star is too massive, the star will no longer be able to support itself and will undergo gravitational collapse.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-196" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-196"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-197" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-197"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>195<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The mass of a black hole formed via a supernova has a lower bound: if the progenitor star is too small, the collapse may be stopped by the <a href="/wiki/Degenerate_matter" title="Degenerate matter">degeneracy pressure</a> of the star's constituents, allowing the condensation of matter into an exotic <a href="/wiki/Degenerate_matter" title="Degenerate matter">denser state</a>. Degeneracy pressure occurs from the <a href="/wiki/Pauli_exclusion_principle" title="Pauli exclusion principle">Pauli exclusion principle</a>: particles will resist being in the same place as each other. Progenitor stars with masses less than about 8&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub> will become <a href="/wiki/White_dwarf" title="White dwarf">white dwarfs</a>, where the degeneracy pressure of electrons balances gravity. For more massive progenitor stars, the force of gravity overcomes electron degeneracy pressure and the star compresses until <a href="/wiki/Neutron_degeneracy_pressure" class="mw-redirect" title="Neutron degeneracy pressure">neutron degeneracy pressure</a> resists gravity, forming a <a href="/wiki/Neutron_star" title="Neutron star">neutron star</a>. If the star is even more massive, neutron degeneracy pressure will not be able to resist the force of gravity and the star will collapse into a black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-198" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-198"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2004&#95;169-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2004-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Location: 5.8">&#58;&#8202;5.8&#8202;</span></sup>
