</p><p>While most of the energy released during gravitational collapse is emitted very quickly, an outside observer does not actually see the end of this process. Even though the collapse takes a finite amount of time from the <a href="/wiki/Frame_of_reference" title="Frame of reference">reference frame</a> of infalling matter, a distant observer would see the infalling material slow and halt just above the event horizon, due to gravitational time dilation. Light from the collapsing material takes longer and longer to reach the observer, with the delay growing to infinity as the emitting material reaches the event horizon. Thus the external observer never sees the formation of the event horizon; instead, the collapsing material seems to become dimmer and increasingly red-shifted, eventually fading away.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-199" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-199"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>197<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Other_mechanisms">Other mechanisms</h3></div>
<p>Observations of quasars from less than a billion years after the <a href="/wiki/Big_Bang" title="Big Bang">Big Bang</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-200" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-200"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>198<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-201" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-201"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>199<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> has led to investigations of other ways to form black holes. The accretion process to build supermassive black holes has a limiting rate of mass accumulation and
a billion years is not enough time to reach quasar status.
One suggestion is <a href="/wiki/Direct_collapse_black_hole" title="Direct collapse black hole">direct collapse</a> of nearly pure hydrogen gas (low metalicity) clouds characteristic of the young universe, forming a supermassive star which collapses into a black hole.
It has been suggested that seed black holes with typical masses of ~10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7000500000000000000♠"></span>5</span></sup>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub> could have formed in this way which then could grow to ~10<sup><span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7000900000000000000♠"></span>9</span></sup>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>. However, the very large amount of gas required for direct collapse is not typically stable against fragmentation which would form multiple stars. Thus another approach suggests massive star formation followed by collisions that seed massive black holes which ultimately merge to create a quasar.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-202" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-202"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>200<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 85">&#58;&#8202;85&#8202;</span></sup>
</p><p>A neutron star in a <a href="/wiki/Common_envelope" title="Common envelope">common envelope</a> with a regular star can accrete sufficient material to collapse to a black hole or two neutron stars can merge. These avenues for the formation of black holes are considered relatively rare.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-203" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-203"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>201<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Primordial_black_holes_and_the_Big_Bang">Primordial black holes and the Big Bang</h3></div>
<p>In the current epoch of the universe, conditions needed to form black holes are rare and are mostly only found in stars. However, in the early universe, conditions may have allowed for black hole formations via other means. Fluctuations of spacetime soon after the Big Bang may have formed areas that were denser than their surroundings. Initially, these regions would not have been compact enough to form a black hole, but eventually, the curvature of spacetime in the regions become large enough to cause them to collapse into a black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-204" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-204"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>202<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-205" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-205"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>203<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Different models for the early universe vary widely in their predictions of the scale of these fluctuations. Various models predict the creation of primordial black holes ranging from a <a href="/wiki/Planck_mass" class="mw-redirect" title="Planck mass">Planck mass</a> (~<span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="6992220000000000000♠"></span>2.2<span style="margin-left:0.25em;margin-right:0.15em;">×</span>10<sup>−8</sup>&#160;kg</span>) to hundreds of thousands of solar masses.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-carr&#95;primordial&#95;206-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-carr_primordial-206"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>204<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-207" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-207"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Primordial black holes with masses less than <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7012100000000000000♠"></span>10<sup>12</sup>&#160;kg</span> would have evaporated by now due to Hawking radiation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carr&#95;91-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carr-91"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Despite the early universe being extremely <a href="/wiki/Density" title="Density">dense</a>, it did not re-collapse into a black hole during the Big Bang, since the universe was expanding rapidly and did not have the gravitational differential necessary for black hole formation. Models for the gravitational collapse of objects of relatively constant size, such as <a href="/wiki/Star" title="Star">stars</a>, do not necessarily apply in the same way to rapidly expanding space such as the Big Bang.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-208" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="High-energy_collisions">High-energy collisions</h3></div>
<p>In principle, black holes could be formed in <a href="/wiki/High-energy_physics" class="mw-redirect" title="High-energy physics">high-energy</a> particle collisions that achieve sufficient density, although no such events have been detected.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-209" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-209"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>207<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-210" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
These hypothetical <a href="/wiki/Micro_black_hole" title="Micro black hole">micro black holes</a>, which could form from the collision of <a href="/wiki/Cosmic_ray" title="Cosmic ray">cosmic rays</a> and Earth's atmosphere or in <a href="/wiki/Particle_accelerator" title="Particle accelerator">particle accelerators</a> like the <a href="/wiki/Large_Hadron_Collider" title="Large Hadron Collider">Large Hadron Collider</a>, would not be able to aggregate additional mass.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-LHCsafety&#95;211-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-LHCsafety-211"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>209<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Instead, they would <a href="/wiki/Black_hole_evaporation" class="mw-redirect" title="Black hole evaporation">evaporate</a> in about 10<sup>−25</sup> seconds, posing no threat to the Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-212" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-212"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>210<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Evolution">Evolution</h2></div>
<p>After a black hole forms, it may change through phenomena such as <a href="/wiki/Black_hole_merger" class="mw-redirect" title="Black hole merger">mergers</a>, <a href="/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics)" title="Accretion (astrophysics)">accretion of matter</a>, and evaporation via <a href="/wiki/Hawking_radiation" title="Hawking radiation">Hawking radiation</a>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Merger">Merger</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_3" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm/250px--BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="141" data-durationhint="35" data-mwtitle="BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a4/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="854" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a4/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm.1080p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="1080p.vp9.webm" data-width="1920" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a4/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp8&quot;" data-width="1920" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a4/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="256" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a4/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm/BBH_gravitational_lensing_of_gw150914.webm.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="426" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Simulation of two black holes colliding</figcaption></figure>
<p>Black holes can merge with other objects such as stars or <a href="/wiki/Binary_black_hole" title="Binary black hole">other black holes</a>. This is thought to have been important, especially in the early growth of supermassive black holes, which could have formed from the aggregation of many smaller objects.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ReesVolonteri&#95;213-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ReesVolonteri-213"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>211<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The process has also been proposed as the origin of some intermediate-mass black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-214" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-214"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>212<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-215" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-215"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>213<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mergers of supermassive black holes may take a long time: As a binary of supermassive black holes approach each other, most nearby stars are <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_slingshot" class="mw-redirect" title="Gravitational slingshot">slingshotted</a> away, leaving little for the black holes to gravitationally interact with that would allow them to get closer to each other. This phenomenon has been called the <a href="/wiki/Final_parsec_problem" class="mw-redirect" title="Final parsec problem">final parsec problem</a>, as the distance at which this happens is usually around one <a href="/wiki/Parsec" title="Parsec">parsec</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-216" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-216"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>214<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-217" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-217"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>215<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Accretion_of_matter">Accretion of matter</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Accretion_disk" title="Accretion disk">Accretion disk</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Centaurus_A-_A_Nearby_Elliptical_Galaxy_With_An_Active_Galactic_Nucleus_(2001-0157blue_-_0157blue_xray).tiff" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="An image, all blue, of Centaurus A&#39;s active galactic nucleus as a bright spot in the center with a bright relativistic jet going away from it." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Centaurus_A-_A_Nearby_Elliptical_Galaxy_With_An_Active_Galactic_Nucleus_%282001-0157blue_-_0157blue_xray%29.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Centaurus_A-_A_Nearby_Elliptical_Galaxy_With_An_Active_Galactic_Nucleus_%282001-0157blue_-_0157blue_xray%29.tiff.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="179" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Centaurus_A-_A_Nearby_Elliptical_Galaxy_With_An_Active_Galactic_Nucleus_%282001-0157blue_-_0157blue_xray%29.tiff/lossy-page1-500px-Centaurus_A-_A_Nearby_Elliptical_Galaxy_With_An_Active_Galactic_Nucleus_%282001-0157blue_-_0157blue_xray%29.tiff.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2154" data-file-height="1539" /></a><figcaption>The active galactic nucleus of galaxy <a href="/wiki/Centaurus_A" title="Centaurus A">Centaurus A</a> in X-ray light, believed to be powered by a supermassive black hole (centre) and surrounded by x-ray binaries (blue dots)</figcaption></figure>
<p>When a black hole <a href="/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics)" title="Accretion (astrophysics)">accretes</a> matter, the gas in the inner accretion disk orbits at very high speeds because of its proximity to the black hole. The resulting <a href="/wiki/Friction" title="Friction">friction</a> heats the inner disk to temperatures at which it emits vast amounts of electromagnetic radiation (mainly <a href="/wiki/X-ray" title="X-ray">X-rays</a>) detectable by telescopes. By the time the matter of the disk reaches the <a href="/wiki/Innermost_stable_circular_orbit" title="Innermost stable circular orbit">ISCO</a>, between 5.7% and 42% of its <a href="/wiki/Mass-energy_equivalence" class="mw-redirect" title="Mass-energy equivalence">mass will have been converted to energy</a>, depending on the black hole's spin. About 90% of this energy is released within 20 black hole radii.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-McClintockRemillard2006&#95;218-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-McClintockRemillard2006-218"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>216<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In many cases, accretion disks are accompanied by <a href="/wiki/Relativistic_jets" class="mw-redirect" title="Relativistic jets">relativistic jets</a> that are emitted along the black hole's poles, which carry away much of the energy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-219" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-219"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>217<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many of the universe's most energetic phenomena have been attributed to the accretion of matter on black holes. <a href="/wiki/Active_galactic_nuclei" class="mw-redirect" title="Active galactic nuclei">Active galactic nuclei</a> and <a href="/wiki/Quasar" title="Quasar">quasars</a> are powered by accretion onto supermassive black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-220" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-220"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>218<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-221" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-221"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>219<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/X-ray_binaries" class="mw-redirect" title="X-ray binaries">X-ray binaries</a> are generally accepted to be <a href="/wiki/Binary_star" title="Binary star">binary</a> systems in which one of the two objects is a <a href="/wiki/Compact_object" title="Compact object">compact object</a> accreting matter from its companion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CMS1999&#95;161-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CMS1999-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Ultraluminous_X-ray_source" title="Ultraluminous X-ray source">Ultraluminous X-ray sources</a> may be the accretion disks of intermediate-mass black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-222" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-222"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>220<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>At a certain rate of accretion, the outward radiation pressure will become as strong as the inward gravitational force, and the black hole should, in theory, be unable to accrete any faster. This limit is called the <a href="/wiki/Eddington_limit" class="mw-redirect" title="Eddington limit">Eddington limit</a>. Realistically, many black holes accrete beyond this rate due to their non-spherical geometry or instabilities in the accretion disk. Accretion beyond the limit is called <a href="/wiki/Super-Eddington_accretion" class="mw-redirect" title="Super-Eddington accretion">super-Eddington accretion</a> and may have been commonplace in the early universe.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-223" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-223"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>221<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Regan&#95;224-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Regan-224"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>222<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Stars have been observed to get torn apart by tidal forces in the immediate vicinity of supermassive black holes in galaxy nuclei, in what is known as a <a href="/wiki/Tidal_disruption_event" title="Tidal disruption event">tidal disruption event</a> (TDE). Some of the material from the disrupted star forms an accretion disk around the black hole, which emits observable electromagnetic radiation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-225" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-225"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>223<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-226" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-226"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Interaction_with_galaxies">Interaction with galaxies</h3></div>
<p>The correlation between the masses of supermassive black holes in the centres of galaxies with the <a href="/wiki/M-sigma_relation" class="mw-redirect" title="M-sigma relation">velocity dispersion</a> and mass of stars in their <a href="/wiki/Galactic_bulge" title="Galactic bulge">host bulges</a> suggests that the formation of galaxies and the formation of their central black holes are related. Black hole <a href="/wiki/Cosmic_wind" title="Cosmic wind">winds</a> from rapid accretion, particularly when the galaxy itself is still accreting matter, can compress gas nearby, accelerating star formation. However, if the winds become too strong, the black hole may blow nearly all of the gas out of the galaxy, quenching star formation. Black hole jets may also energise nearby <a href="/wiki/Stellar-wind_bubble" title="Stellar-wind bubble">cavities</a> of plasma and eject low-<a href="/wiki/Entropy" title="Entropy">entropy</a> gas from out of the galactic core, causing gas in galactic centers to be <a href="/wiki/Cooling_flow#Cooling_flow_problem" title="Cooling flow">hotter than expected</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-227" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-227"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>225<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Evaporation">Evaporation</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Hawking_radiation" title="Hawking radiation">Hawking radiation</a></div>
<p>If Hawking's theory of black hole radiation is correct, then black holes are expected to shrink and evaporate over time as they lose mass by the emission of photons and other particles.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Hawking1974&#95;43-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hawking1974-43"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The temperature of this thermal spectrum (<a href="/wiki/Hawking_temperature" class="mw-redirect" title="Hawking temperature">Hawking temperature</a>) is proportional to the surface gravity of the black hole, which is inversely proportional to the mass. Hence, large black holes emit less radiation than small black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carroll-2004&#95;169-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carroll-2004-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Location: Ch. 9.6">&#58;&#8202;Ch. 9.6&#8202;</span></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-228" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-228"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>226<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A stellar black hole of 1&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub> has a Hawking temperature of 62&#160;<a href="/wiki/Nanokelvin" class="mw-redirect" title="Nanokelvin">nanokelvins</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-229" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-229"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>227<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This is far less than the 2.7&#160;K temperature of the <a href="/wiki/Cosmic_microwave_background" title="Cosmic microwave background">cosmic microwave background</a> radiation. Stellar-mass or larger black holes receive more mass from the cosmic microwave background than they emit through Hawking radiation and thus will grow instead of shrinking.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-230" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-230"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>228<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> To have a Hawking temperature larger than 2.7&#160;K (and be able to evaporate), a black hole would need a mass less than the <a href="/wiki/Moon" title="Moon">Moon</a>. Such a black hole would have a diameter of less than a tenth of a millimetre.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-231" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-231"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>229<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The Hawking radiation for an astrophysical black hole is predicted to be very weak and would thus be exceedingly difficult to detect from Earth. A possible exception is the <a href="/wiki/Microsecond" title="Microsecond">microsecond</a>-long burst of <a href="/wiki/Gamma_ray" title="Gamma ray">gamma rays</a> emitted in the last stage of the evaporation of primordial black holes. Searches for such flashes have proven unsuccessful and provide stringent limits on the possibility of existence of low mass primordial black holes, with modern research predicting that primordial black holes must make up less than a fraction of <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="6993100000000000000♠"></span>10<sup>−7</sup></span> of the universe's total mass.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-232" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-232"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>230<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carr&#95;91-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carr-91"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NASA's <a href="/wiki/Fermi_Gamma-ray_Space_Telescope" title="Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope">Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope</a>, launched in 2008, has searched for these flashes, but has not yet found any.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-233" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-233"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-234" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-234"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Laws_of_mechanics_and_thermodynamics">Laws of mechanics and thermodynamics</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Black_hole_thermodynamics" title="Black hole thermodynamics">Black hole thermodynamics</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Bekenstein-Hawking_entropy_of_a_black_hole.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A geodesic sphere made up of triangles labelled as the black hole event horizon. Each triangle is labelled as &quot;one Planck area&quot; and &quot;one unit of entropy&quot;." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Bekenstein-Hawking_entropy_of_a_black_hole.svg/250px-Bekenstein-Hawking_entropy_of_a_black_hole.svg.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="171" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Bekenstein-Hawking_entropy_of_a_black_hole.svg/500px-Bekenstein-Hawking_entropy_of_a_black_hole.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="677" data-file-height="463" /></a><figcaption>A black hole's entropy <a href="/wiki/Direct_proportionality" class="mw-redirect" title="Direct proportionality">scales</a> with the surface area of its event horizon.</figcaption></figure>
<p>When based in general relativity, the constraints on a black hole's properties are called the <a href="/wiki/Laws_of_black_hole_mechanics" class="mw-redirect" title="Laws of black hole mechanics">laws of black hole mechanics</a>. For a black hole that is not still forming or accreting matter, the zeroth law of black hole mechanics states the black hole's <a href="/wiki/Surface_gravity" title="Surface gravity">surface gravity</a> is constant across the event horizon. The first law relates changes in the black hole's surface area, angular momentum, and charge to changes in its energy. The second law says the surface area of a black hole never decreases on its own. Finally, the third law says that the surface gravity of a black hole is never zero. These laws are mathematical analogues of the <a href="/wiki/Laws_of_thermodynamics" title="Laws of thermodynamics">laws of thermodynamics</a>. They are not equivalent, however, because, according to general relativity without quantum mechanics, a black hole can never emit radiation, and thus its temperature must always be zero.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-WaldLiving&#95;235-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-WaldLiving-235"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 11">&#58;&#8202;11&#8202;</span></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-carlip14&#95;236-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-carlip14-236"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Quantum mechanics predicts that a black hole will continuously emit thermal Hawking radiation, and therefore must always have a nonzero temperature. It also predicts that all black holes have <a href="/wiki/Entropy" title="Entropy">entropy</a> which scales with their surface area. When quantum mechanics is accounted for, the laws of black hole mechanics become equivalent to the classical laws of thermodynamics.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-WaldLiving&#95;235-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-WaldLiving-235"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-237" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-237"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>235<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, these conclusions are derived without a complete theory of quantum gravity, although many potential theories do predict black holes having entropy and temperature. Thus, the true quantum nature of black hole thermodynamics continues to be debated.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-WaldLiving&#95;235-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-WaldLiving-235"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 29">&#58;&#8202;29&#8202;</span></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-carlip14&#95;236-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-carlip14-236"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Observational_evidence">Observational evidence</h2></div>
<p>Millions of black holes derived from stellar collapse are expected to exist in the Milky Way. Even a <a href="/wiki/Dwarf_galaxy" title="Dwarf galaxy">dwarf galaxy</a> like <a href="/wiki/Draco_(dwarf_galaxy)" class="mw-redirect" title="Draco (dwarf galaxy)">Draco</a> should have hundreds.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-238" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-238"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>236<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Only a few of these have been detected.
By nature, black holes do not themselves emit any electromagnetic radiation other than the hypothetical, typically extremely weak Hawking radiation, so astrophysicists searching for black holes must rely on indirect observations. The defining characteristic of a black hole is its event horizon. The horizon itself cannot be imaged,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-239" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-239"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>237<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> so all other possible explanations for these indirect observations must be considered and eliminated before concluding that a black hole has been observed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-240" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-240"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>238<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 11">&#58;&#8202;11&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Direct_interferometry">Direct interferometry</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:248px;max-width:248px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:122px;max-width:122px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:A_view_of_the_M87_supermassive_black_hole_in_polarised_light.tif" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="An orange donut of gases surrounding a black hole, with lines superimposed over the gases" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/A_view_of_the_M87_supermassive_black_hole_in_polarised_light.tif/lossy-page1-120px-A_view_of_the_M87_supermassive_black_hole_in_polarised_light.tif.jpg" decoding="async" width="120" height="121" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/A_view_of_the_M87_supermassive_black_hole_in_polarised_light.tif/lossy-page1-250px-A_view_of_the_M87_supermassive_black_hole_in_polarised_light.tif.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3414" data-file-height="3447" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">An <a href="/wiki/M87*" class="mw-redirect" title="M87*">M87*</a> image with superimposed lines representing the magnitude and direction of polarisation</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:122px;max-width:122px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:A_view_of_the_jet_and_shadow_of_M87%E2%80%99s_black_hole_(eso2305a).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="M87* with a long relativistic jet extending from it. The Event Horizon Telescope image of the black hole is inset." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/A_view_of_the_jet_and_shadow_of_M87%E2%80%99s_black_hole_%28eso2305a%29.jpg/120px-A_view_of_the_jet_and_shadow_of_M87%E2%80%99s_black_hole_%28eso2305a%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="120" height="93" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/A_view_of_the_jet_and_shadow_of_M87%E2%80%99s_black_hole_%28eso2305a%29.jpg/250px-A_view_of_the_jet_and_shadow_of_M87%E2%80%99s_black_hole_%28eso2305a%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3300" data-file-height="2550" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">The M87* relativistic jet; inset is the black hole shadow</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Event_Horizon_Telescope" title="Event Horizon Telescope">Event Horizon Telescope</a> (EHT) is a global system of radio telescopes capable of directly observing a black hole shadow.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-APJL-20190410&#95;56-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-APJL-20190410-56"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Angular_resolution" title="Angular resolution">angular resolution</a> of a telescope is based on its <a href="/wiki/Aperture" title="Aperture">aperture</a> and the wavelengths it is observing. Because the <a href="/wiki/Angular_diameter" title="Angular diameter">angular diameters</a> of Sagittarius A* and Messier 87* in the sky are very small, a single telescope would need to be about the size of the Earth to clearly distinguish their horizons using radio wavelengths. By combining data from several different radio telescopes around the world, the Event Horizon Telescope creates an effective aperture the diameter size of the Earth. The EHT team used <a href="/wiki/CLEAN_(algorithm)" title="CLEAN (algorithm)">imaging algorithms</a> to compute the most probable image from the data in its <a href="/wiki/History_of_black_hole_physics#EHT" title="History of black hole physics">observations of Sagittarius A* and M87*</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-241" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-241"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>239<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-eht19&#95;1-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eht19-1"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Gravitational_waves">Gravitational waves</h3></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Gravitational-wave_observatory" title="Gravitational-wave observatory">Gravitational-wave interferometry</a> can be used to detect merging black holes and other compact objects. In this method, a laser beam is split, sent down two long arms of a tunnel, then reflected at the far end of the tunnels to reconverge at the intersection of the arms, precisely <a href="/wiki/Destructive_interference" class="mw-redirect" title="Destructive interference">cancelling each other</a>. However, when a gravitational wave passes, it warps spacetime, changing the relative lengths of the arms themselves. Since each laser beam is now travelling a slightly different distance, they do not cancel out and produce a recognisable signal. Analysis of the signal can give scientists information about what caused the gravitational waves. Since gravitational waves are very weak, gravitational-wave observatories such as <a href="/wiki/LIGO" title="LIGO">LIGO</a> must have arms several kilometres long and carefully control for <a href="/wiki/Seismic_noise" title="Seismic noise">noise</a> from Earth to be able to detect these gravitational waves.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-242" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-242"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>240<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since <a href="/wiki/History_of_black_hole_physics#LIGO" title="History of black hole physics">the first measurements in 2016</a>, multiple gravitational waves from black holes have been detected and analysed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-reynolds21&#95;105-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-reynolds21-105"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>105<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Stars_orbiting_Sagittarius_A*"><span id="Stars_orbiting_Sagittarius_A.2A"></span>Stars orbiting Sagittarius A*</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Sagittarius_A*_cluster" title="Sagittarius A* cluster">Sagittarius A* cluster</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_4" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/SgrA2021_video.ogg/250px--SgrA2021_video.ogg.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="279" data-durationhint="12" data-mwtitle="SgrA2021_video.ogg" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:SgrA2021_video.ogg"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/3/38/SgrA2021_video.ogg/SgrA2021_video.ogg.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="430" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/38/SgrA2021_video.ogg" type="video/ogg; codecs=&quot;theora&quot;" data-width="490" data-height="546" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/3/38/SgrA2021_video.ogg/SgrA2021_video.ogg.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="130" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/3/38/SgrA2021_video.ogg/SgrA2021_video.ogg.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="216" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Stars moving around Sagittarius A*, as seen in 2021</figcaption></figure>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Proper_motion" title="Proper motion">proper motions</a> of stars near the centre of the Milky Way provide strong observational evidence that these stars are orbiting a supermassive black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Gillessen&#95;243-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Gillessen-243"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>241<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Astronomers have tracked the motions of 90 stars orbiting an invisible object coincident with the radio source Sagittarius A*. One of the stars—called <a href="/wiki/S2_(star)" title="S2 (star)">S2</a>—completed a full orbit.  By fitting the motions of stars to <a href="/wiki/Keplerian_orbit" class="mw-redirect" title="Keplerian orbit">Keplerian orbits</a>, the astronomers were able to infer that the invisible object assumed to be Sagittarius A* must have a mass of <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7006430000000000000♠"></span>4.3<span style="margin-left:0.25em;margin-right:0.15em;">×</span>10<sup>6</sup></span>&#160;<i>M</i><sub>☉</sub>, with a radius of less than 0.002 light-years.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Gillessen&#95;243-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Gillessen-243"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>241<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This upper limit radius is larger than the Schwarzschild radius for the estimated mass, so the combination does not prove Sagittarius A* is a black hole. Nevertheless, these observations strongly suggest that the central object is a supermassive black hole as there are no other plausible scenarios for confining so much invisible mass into such a small volume.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ghez1998&#95;51-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ghez1998-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Additionally, luminosity data from this object implies it must possess an event horizon, a defining feature of black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-244" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-244"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>242<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Event Horizon Telescope image of Sagittarius A*, released in 2022, provided further confirmation that it is indeed a black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-eht-press-release&#95;57-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eht-press-release-57"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Binaries">Binaries</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/X-ray_binary" title="X-ray binary">X-ray binary</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Chandra_image_of_Cygnus_X-1.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Black hole Cygnus X-1 as seen by the Chandra X-Ray Observatory, a bright spot over a black background." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Chandra_image_of_Cygnus_X-1.jpg/250px-Chandra_image_of_Cygnus_X-1.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Chandra_image_of_Cygnus_X-1.jpg/500px-Chandra_image_of_Cygnus_X-1.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2400" data-file-height="2400" /></a><figcaption>A <a href="/wiki/Chandra_X-Ray_Observatory" class="mw-redirect" title="Chandra X-Ray Observatory">Chandra X-Ray Observatory</a> image of <a href="/wiki/Cygnus_X-1" title="Cygnus X-1">Cygnus X-1</a>, which was the first strong black hole candidate discovered</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/X-ray_binaries" class="mw-redirect" title="X-ray binaries">X-ray binaries</a> are binary systems that emit a majority of their radiation in the <a href="/wiki/X-ray" title="X-ray">X-ray</a> part of the <a href="/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum" title="Electromagnetic spectrum">electromagnetic spectrum</a>. These X-ray emissions result when a compact object accretes matter from an ordinary star.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-245" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-245"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The presence of an ordinary star in such a system provides an opportunity for studying the central object and to determine if it might be a black hole. By measuring the <a href="/wiki/Orbital_period" title="Orbital period">orbital period</a> of the binary, the distance to the binary from Earth, and the mass of the companion star, scientists can estimate the mass of the compact object.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-246" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-246"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>244<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff_limit" class="mw-redirect" title="Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff limit">Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff limit</a> (TOV limit) dictates the largest mass a nonrotating neutron star can be, and is estimated to be about two solar masses. While a rotating neutron star can be slightly more massive, if the compact object is much more massive than the TOV limit, it cannot be a neutron star and is generally expected to be a black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CMS1999&#95;161-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CMS1999-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-247" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-247"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>245<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The first strong candidate for a black hole, <a href="/wiki/Cygnus_X-1" title="Cygnus X-1">Cygnus X-1</a>, was discovered in this way by <a href="/wiki/Charles_Thomas_Bolton" class="mw-redirect" title="Charles Thomas Bolton">Charles Thomas Bolton</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bolton1972&#95;248-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bolton1972-248"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>246<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Louise_Webster" class="mw-redirect" title="Louise Webster">Louise Webster</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Paul_Murdin" title="Paul Murdin">Paul Murdin</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Webster1972&#95;249-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Webster1972-249"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> in 1972.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-250" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-250"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>248<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Shipman1975&#95;41-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Shipman1975-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>  Observations of rotation broadening of the optical star reported in 1986 lead to a compact object mass estimate of 16 solar masses, with 7 solar masses as the lower bound.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CMS1999&#95;161-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CMS1999-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2011, this estimate was updated to <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7031280385550000000♠"></span>14.1<span style="margin-left:0.225em;margin-right:0.225em;">±</span>1.0&#160;<i>M</i><sub>&#x2609;</sub></span> for the black hole and <span class="nowrap"><span data-sort-value="7031381801600000000♠"></span>19.2<span style="margin-left:0.225em;margin-right:0.225em;">±</span>1.9&#160;<i>M</i><sub>&#x2609;</sub></span> for the optical stellar companion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-251" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-251"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/X-ray_binaries" class="mw-redirect" title="X-ray binaries">X-ray binaries</a> can be categorised as either <em>low-mass</em> or <em>high-mass</em>; This classification is based on the mass of the companion star, not the compact object itself.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lph97&#95;95-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lph97-95"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In a class of X-ray binaries called soft X-ray transients, the companion star is of relatively low mass, allowing for more accurate estimates of the black hole mass. These systems actively emit X-rays for only several months once every 10–50 years. During the period of low X-ray emission, called quiescence, the accretion disk is extremely faint, allowing detailed observation of the companion star.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CMS1999&#95;161-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CMS1999-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Numerous black hole candidates have been measured by this method.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-252" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-252"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>250<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Black holes are also sometimes found in binaries with other compact objects, such as <a href="/wiki/White_dwarf" title="White dwarf">white dwarfs</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lph97&#95;95-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lph97-95"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> neutron stars,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-253" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-253"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>251<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-254" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>252<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and other black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-255" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>253<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-256" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-256"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>254<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Galactic_nuclei">Galactic nuclei</h3></div>
<p>The centre of nearly every galaxy contains a supermassive black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-257" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-257"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>255<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The close observational correlation between the mass of this hole and the velocity dispersion of the host galaxy's <a href="/wiki/Galactic_bulge" title="Galactic bulge">bulge</a>, known as the <a href="/wiki/M%E2%80%93sigma_relation" title="M–sigma relation">M–sigma relation</a>, strongly suggests a connection between the formation of the black hole and that of the galaxy itself.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-King&#95;258-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-King-258"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>256<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-msigma2000&#95;259-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-msigma2000-259"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>257<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Active_galactic_nucleus">Active galactic nucleus</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Active_galactic_nucleus" title="Active galactic nucleus">Active galactic nucleus</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:X-RayFlare-BlackHole-MilkyWay-20140105.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="The center of the Milky Way, with an inset X-ray image of Sagittarius A*" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/X-RayFlare-BlackHole-MilkyWay-20140105.jpg/250px-X-RayFlare-BlackHole-MilkyWay-20140105.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/X-RayFlare-BlackHole-MilkyWay-20140105.jpg/500px-X-RayFlare-BlackHole-MilkyWay-20140105.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="1200" /></a><figcaption>Detection of an unusually bright <a href="/wiki/X-ray" title="X-ray">X-ray</a> flare from Sagittarius A*, a black hole in the centre of the Milky Way galaxy on 5<span class="nowrap">&#160;</span>January 2015<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA-20150105&#95;260-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA-20150105-260"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>258<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Astronomers use the term <i>active galaxy</i> to describe galaxies with unusual characteristics, such as unusual <a href="/wiki/Spectral_line" title="Spectral line">spectral line</a> emission and very strong radio emission. Theoretical and observational studies have shown that the high levels of activity in the centers of these galaxies, regions called active galactic nuclei (AGN), may be explained by accretion onto supermassive black holes. These AGN consist of a central black hole that may be millions or billions of times more massive than the <a href="/wiki/Sun" title="Sun">Sun</a>, a disk of <a href="/wiki/Interstellar_gas" class="mw-redirect" title="Interstellar gas">interstellar gas</a> and dust called an accretion disk, and two <a href="/wiki/Relativistic_jet" class="mw-redirect" title="Relativistic jet">jets</a> perpendicular to the accretion disk.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-261" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-261"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>259<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Although supermassive black holes are expected to be found in most AGN, only some galaxies' nuclei have been more carefully studied in attempts to both identify and measure the actual masses of the central supermassive black hole candidates. Some of the most notable galaxies with supermassive black hole candidates include the <a href="/wiki/Andromeda_Galaxy" title="Andromeda Galaxy">Andromeda Galaxy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Messier_32" title="Messier 32">Messier 32</a>, <a href="/wiki/Messier_87" title="Messier 87">Messier 87</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Sombrero_Galaxy" title="Sombrero Galaxy">Sombrero Galaxy</a>, and the Milky Way itself.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-kormendyrichstone1995&#95;262-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-kormendyrichstone1995-262"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>260<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-263" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-263"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>261<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Microlensing">Microlensing</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Gravitational_microlensing_by_black_hole_-_cropped.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A diagram of gravitational microlensing: A foreground black hole warps light from a background star, creating two images of the star. The light then travels to Earth-based telescopes, where the two images cannot be resolved and appear solely as a single brightened star." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Gravitational_microlensing_by_black_hole_-_cropped.jpg/250px-Gravitational_microlensing_by_black_hole_-_cropped.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="146" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Gravitational_microlensing_by_black_hole_-_cropped.jpg/500px-Gravitational_microlensing_by_black_hole_-_cropped.jpg 2x" data-file-width="585" data-file-height="342" /></a><figcaption>The intense gravitational field of a foreground black hole acts like a powerful lens, distorting and brightening the image of a background star.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Another way black holes can be detected is through observation of effects caused by their strong gravitational field. One such effect is <a href="/wiki/Gravitational_lensing" class="mw-redirect" title="Gravitational lensing">gravitational lensing</a>: the deformation of spacetime around a massive object causes light rays to be deflected, making objects behind them appear distorted.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-264" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-264"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>262<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> When the lensing object is a black hole, this effect can be strong enough to create multiple images of a star or other luminous source.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bm05&#95;265-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bm05-265"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>263<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, the distance between the lensed images may be too small for contemporary telescopes to <a href="/wiki/Angular_resolution" title="Angular resolution">resolve</a>—this phenomenon is called <a href="/wiki/Microlensing" class="mw-redirect" title="Microlensing">microlensing</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-266" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-266"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>264<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Instead of seeing two images of a lensed star, astronomers see the star brighten slightly as the black hole moves towards the <a href="/wiki/Line_of_sight" title="Line of sight">line of sight</a> between the star and Earth and then return to its normal luminosity as the black hole moves away.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-267" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-267"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>265<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The first three candidate black holes detected in this way were found around the turn of the millennium.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-268" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-268"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-269" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-269"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>267<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In January 2022, astronomers reported the first confirmed detection of an <i>isolated</i> stellar black hole—a black hole with no binary partner—and its mass; The black hole was found via detection of microlensing by the <a href="/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope" title="Hubble Space Telescope">Hubble Space Telescope</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Sahu&#95;270-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sahu-270"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>268<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-271" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-271"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>269<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Areas_of_investigation">Areas of investigation</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Information_loss_paradox">Information loss paradox</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Black_hole_information_paradox" title="Black hole information paradox">Black hole information paradox</a></div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1287834656">.mw-parser-output .unsolved{margin:0.5em 0 1em 1em;border:1px solid #a2a9b1;padding:0.35em 0.35em 0.35em 2.2em;background-color:var(--background-color-interactive-subtle);background-image:url("https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/26/Question%2C_Web_Fundamentals.svg");background-position:top 50%left 0.35em;background-size:1.5em;background-repeat:no-repeat}@media(min-width:720px){.mw-parser-output .unsolved{clear:right;float:right;max-width:25%}}.mw-parser-output .unsolved-label{font-weight:bold}.mw-parser-output .unsolved-body{margin:0.35em;font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output .unsolved-more{font-size:smaller}</style>
<div role="note" aria-labelledby="unsolved-label-physics" class="unsolved">
<div><span class="unsolved-label" id="unsolved-label-physics">Unsolved problem in physics</span></div>
<div class="unsolved-body">Is physical information lost in black holes?</div>
<div class="unsolved-more"><a href="/wiki/List_of_unsolved_problems_in_physics" title="List of unsolved problems in physics">More unsolved problems in physics</a></div>
</div>
<p>According to the no-hair theorem, a black hole is defined by only three parameters: its mass, charge, and angular momentum. This seems to mean that all other information about the matter that went into forming the black hole is lost, as there is no way to determine anything about the black hole from outside other than those three parameters. When black holes were thought to persist forever, this information loss was not problematic, as the information can be thought of as existing inside the black hole. However, black holes slowly evaporate by emitting Hawking radiation. This radiation does not appear to carry any additional information about the matter that formed the black hole, meaning that this information is seemingly gone forever. This is called the <a href="/wiki/Black_hole_information_paradox" title="Black hole information paradox">black hole information paradox</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-PlayDice000&#95;272-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-PlayDice000-272"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>270<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-math&#95;ucr&#95;edu&#95;273-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-math_ucr_edu-273"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>271<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Preskill1994&#95;274-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Preskill1994-274"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>272<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Theoretical studies analysing the paradox have led to both further paradoxes and new ideas about the intersection of quantum mechanics and general relativity. While there is no consensus on the resolution of the paradox, work on the problem is expected to be important for a theory of <a href="/wiki/Quantum_gravity" title="Quantum gravity">quantum gravity</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-275" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 126">&#58;&#8202;126&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Supermassive_black_holes_in_the_early_universe">Supermassive black holes in the early universe</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:High-Redshift_Quasar_and_Companion_Galaxy_(Illustration)_(2020-51-4755-Image).png" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Two spiral galaxies, one of which has a very bright center" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/High-Redshift_Quasar_and_Companion_Galaxy_%28Illustration%29_%282020-51-4755-Image%29.png/250px-High-Redshift_Quasar_and_Companion_Galaxy_%28Illustration%29_%282020-51-4755-Image%29.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/High-Redshift_Quasar_and_Companion_Galaxy_%28Illustration%29_%282020-51-4755-Image%29.png/500px-High-Redshift_Quasar_and_Companion_Galaxy_%28Illustration%29_%282020-51-4755-Image%29.png 2x" data-file-width="3840" data-file-height="2160" /></a><figcaption>Two galaxies from the first billion years after the Big Bang. The galaxy on the left hosts a luminous quasar at its center.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Observations of faraway galaxies have found that ultraluminous quasars, powered by supermassive black holes, existed in the early universe as far as redshift <span class="mwe-math-element mwe-math-element-inline"><span class="mwe-math-mathml-inline mwe-math-mathml-a11y" style="display: none;"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"  alttext="{\displaystyle z\geq 7}">
  <semantics>
    <mrow class="MJX-TeXAtom-ORD">
      <mstyle displaystyle="true" scriptlevel="0">
        <mi>z</mi>
        <mo>&#x2265;<!-- ≥ --></mo>
        <mn>7</mn>
      </mstyle>
    </mrow>
    <annotation encoding="application/x-tex">{\displaystyle z\geq 7}</annotation>
  </semantics>
</math></span><img src="https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/18c94cb82dcc4fb43c0635d71eb51a362fee5b92" class="mwe-math-fallback-image-inline mw-invert skin-invert" aria-hidden="true" style="vertical-align: -0.505ex; width:5.349ex; height:2.343ex;" alt="{\displaystyle z\geq 7}"></span>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-276" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-276"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>274<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These black holes have been assumed to be the products of the gravitational collapse of large <a href="/wiki/Population_III_star" class="mw-redirect" title="Population III star">population III stars</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-277" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-277"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>275<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-278" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-278"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>276<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, these stellar remnants were not massive enough to produce the quasars observed at early times without accreting beyond the Eddington limit, the theoretical maximum rate of black hole accretion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sb19&#95;279-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sb19-279"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>277<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-280" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-280"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>278<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Physicists have suggested a variety of different mechanisms by which these supermassive black holes may have formed. It has been proposed that smaller black holes may have also undergone mergers to produce the observed supermassive black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-281" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-281"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>279<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-282" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-282"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>280<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is also possible that they were seeded by <a href="/wiki/Direct-collapse_black_hole" class="mw-redirect" title="Direct-collapse black hole">direct-collapse black holes</a>, in which a large cloud of hot gas avoids fragmentation that would lead to multiple stars, due to low angular momentum or heating from a nearby galaxy. Given the right circumstances, a single supermassive star forms and collapses directly into a black hole without undergoing typical <a href="/wiki/Stellar_evolution" title="Stellar evolution">stellar evolution</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-283" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-283"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>281<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-284" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-284"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>282<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Additionally, these supermassive black holes in the early universe may be high-mass primordial black holes, which could have accreted further matter in the centers of galaxies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-285" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-285"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>283<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Finally, certain mechanisms allow black holes to grow faster than the theoretical Eddington limit, such as dense gas in the accretion disk limiting outward radiation pressure that prevents the black hole from accreting.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sb19&#95;279-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sb19-279"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>277<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-286" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-286"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>284<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, the formation of bipolar jets prevent super-Eddington rates.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Regan&#95;224-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Regan-224"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>222<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Alternatives_to_black_holes">Alternatives to black holes</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Exotic_star" title="Exotic star">Exotic star</a></div>
<p>While there is a strong case for supermassive black holes,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-287" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-287"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>285<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-288" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-288"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>286<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the dividing line between lighter black holes and neutron stars relies on theories of extremely dense matter. Direct observational tests are not available: objects observed to have mass higher than the predictions for neutron stars are assumed to be black holes. Recent evidence from gravitational wave events suggests modifications of these theories may be needed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Vink-2021&#95;94-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Vink-2021-94"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> New exotic <a href="/wiki/Phase_(matter)" title="Phase (matter)">phases of matter</a> could allow other kinds of massive objects.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CMS1999&#95;161-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CMS1999-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Quark_star" title="Quark star">Quark stars</a> would be made up of <a href="/wiki/Quark_matter" class="mw-redirect" title="Quark matter">quark matter</a> and supported by quark degeneracy pressure, a form of degeneracy pressure even stronger than neutron degeneracy pressure. This would halt gravitational collapse at a higher mass than for a neutron star.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-289" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-289"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>287<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-290" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-290"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>288<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Even stronger stars called <a href="/wiki/Electroweak_star" class="mw-redirect" title="Electroweak star">electroweak stars</a> would convert quarks in their cores into <a href="/wiki/Lepton" title="Lepton">leptons</a>, providing additional pressure to stop the star from collapsing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bonkowsky25&#95;291-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bonkowsky25-291"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>289<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-292" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-292"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>290<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> If, as some extensions of the <a href="/wiki/Standard_Model" title="Standard Model">Standard Model</a> posit, <a href="/wiki/Quark" title="Quark">quarks</a> and <a href="/wiki/Lepton" title="Lepton">leptons</a> are made up of the even-smaller fundamental particles called <a href="/wiki/Preon" title="Preon">preons</a>, a very compact star could be supported by preon degeneracy pressure.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-293" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-293"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>291<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> While none of these hypothetical models can explain all of the observations of stellar black hole candidates, a <a href="/wiki/Q_star" title="Q star">Q star</a> is the only alternative which could significantly exceed the mass limit for neutron stars and thus provide an alternative for supermassive black holes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CMS1999&#95;161-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CMS1999-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 12">&#58;&#8202;12&#8202;</span></sup>
</p><p>A few theoretical objects have been conjectured to match observations of astronomical black hole candidates identically or near-identically, but which function via a different mechanism.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Murk2023&#95;294-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Murk2023-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>292<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A <a href="/wiki/Dark_energy_star" class="mw-redirect" title="Dark energy star">dark energy star</a> would convert infalling matter into <a href="/wiki/Vacuum_energy" title="Vacuum energy">vacuum energy</a>; This vacuum energy would be much larger than the vacuum energy of outside space, exerting outwards pressure and preventing a singularity from forming.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-295" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-295"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>293<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-296" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-296"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>294<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A <a href="/wiki/Black_star_(semiclassical_gravity)" title="Black star (semiclassical gravity)">black star</a> would be gravitationally collapsing slowly enough that quantum effects would keep it just on the cusp of fully collapsing into a black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-297" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-297"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>295<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A <a href="/wiki/Gravastar" title="Gravastar">gravastar</a> would consist of a very thin shell and a dark-energy interior providing outward pressure to stop the collapse into a black hole or formation of a singularity; It could even have another gravastar inside, called a 'nestar'.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-298" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-298"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>296<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="In_fiction">In fiction</h2></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Interstellar_black_hole_(no_lens_flare).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A black hole over a black background surrounded by a light orange accretion disk. The disk wraps around the top and bottom of the black hole, and across the front like a crossbar. A white, mostly-circular photon sphere is slightly inset inside the black hole&#39;s shadow." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Interstellar_black_hole_%28no_lens_flare%29.jpg/250px-Interstellar_black_hole_%28no_lens_flare%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="113" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Interstellar_black_hole_%28no_lens_flare%29.jpg/500px-Interstellar_black_hole_%28no_lens_flare%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1275" data-file-height="578" /></a><figcaption>The black hole and accretion disk used in the movie <i>Interstellar</i>, without lens flare. Interstellar's visual effects team used relativity to visualize gravitational lensing around the black hole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jtft15&#95;133-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jtft15-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>The concept of black holes have inspired artists as well as scientists.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-299" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-299"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>297<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In her book <i>Conjuring the Void: the Art of Black Holes,</i> <a href="/wiki/Lynn_Gamwell" title="Lynn Gamwell">Lynn Gamwell</a> used black holes as an example to explore how art and science interact, considering the application of art to create scientific visualizations and the impact of scientific ideas on art concepts like darkness.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-300" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-300"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>298<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Fictional treatments of black holes are also used as a mechanism for teaching science.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-kyle19&#95;301-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-kyle19-301"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>299<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FraknoiBlackHoles&#95;302-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FraknoiBlackHoles-302"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>300<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Depictions">Depictions</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Black_holes_in_fiction" title="Black holes in fiction">Black holes in fiction</a></div>
<p>Black holes have been portrayed in science fiction in a variety of ways. Even before the advent of the term itself, objects with characteristics of black holes appeared in stories such as the 1928 novel <i><a href="/wiki/The_Skylark_of_Space" title="The Skylark of Space">The Skylark of Space</a></i> with its "black Sun" and the "hole in space" in the 1935 short story <i><a href="/w/index.php?title=Starship_Invincible&amp;action=edit&amp;redlink=1" class="new" title="Starship Invincible (page does not exist)">Starship Invincible</a></i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-WestfahlBlackHoles&#95;303-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-WestfahlBlackHoles-303"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>301<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Fans of science fiction films typically want the fiction to closely follow the science.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-kyle19&#95;301-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-kyle19-301"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>299<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the 2014 space epic <a href="/wiki/Interstellar_(film)" title="Interstellar (film)"><i>Interstellar</i></a> and the 2018 science fiction film <a href="/wiki/High_Life_(2018_film)" title="High Life (2018 film)"><i>High Life</i></a>, relativity was incorporated into their black hole visualizations, leading to results similar to images derived from the <a href="/wiki/Event_Horizon_Telescope" title="Event Horizon Telescope">Event Horizon Telescope</a>, although both used some <a href="/wiki/Artistic_license" title="Artistic license">artistic license</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-304" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-304"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>302<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-jtft15&#95;133-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jtft15-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Authors and screenwriters have exploited the relativistic effects of black holes, particularly gravitational time dilation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-StablefordBlackHole&#95;305-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-StablefordBlackHole-305"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>303<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For example, <i>Interstellar</i> features a <a href="/wiki/Blanet" title="Blanet">black hole planet</a> with a time dilation factor of over 60,000:1,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-scienceofinterstellar&#95;170-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-scienceofinterstellar-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 163">&#58;&#8202;163&#8202;</span></sup> while the 1977 <a href="/wiki/Frederik_Pohl" title="Frederik Pohl">Pohl</a> novel <i><a href="/wiki/Gateway_(novel)" title="Gateway (novel)">Gateway</a></i> depicts a spaceship approaching but never crossing the event horizon of a black hole from the perspective of an outside observer due to time dilation effects.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-GreenwoodBlackHoles&#95;306-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-GreenwoodBlackHoles-306"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>304<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Black holes have also been appropriated as wormholes or other methods of faster-than-light travel, such as in the 1974 <a href="/wiki/Joe_Haldeman" title="Joe Haldeman">Haldeman</a> novel <i><a href="/wiki/The_Forever_War" title="The Forever War">The Forever War</a></i>, where a network of black holes is used for interstellar travel.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-StablefordBlackHole&#95;305-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-StablefordBlackHole-305"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>303<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Notes">Notes</h2></div>
